output: Mr. President! I warmly welcome the approach outlined in the White Paper, which seeks to identify the root causes of malnutrition and obesity and the resulting diseases.
Unfortunately, in food policy in Europe, regardless of whether we are discussing nutrient profiles, food labelling, or certain aspects in the White Paper, we fall into a very one-sided approach.
We are attempting, in fact, to solve the problems we have in Europe through product policy.
And I believe that it is fundamentally flawed policy to assume that there are good and bad foods.
Many amendment proposals indicate that this does not exist.
There are only good and bad, or likewise balanced and unbalanced, dietary patterns.
That should be our approach.
The causes of incorrect nutrition are multifaceted.

Attempting to address this by trying to guide consumers through product labeling, or even by imposing advertising bans or regulations, or by applying different VAT rates, will not bring us to our goal.

I believe everything we are doing here does not aim to pre-empt the debate on labelling regulations, but everything we are doing here should fall under the heading: What can we do to encourage consumers to reflect on their eating habits?
A traffic light label suggesting to him that I am now facing a good or bad product strikes me as an instrument of disenfranchisement rather than one of education and enlightenment.
Therefore, I wish for a balanced approach here and the understanding that relying solely on product policy will not advance us further.
Mr. President, Madam Vice-President, dear colleagues! The Budgetary Control Committee does not have any new topics.
We are satisfied with the old issues and would also be content with progress on the old topics.
Five points at the top of the agenda: Our urgent appeal for support for Romania and Bulgaria in building the rule of law, the judiciary, and financial oversight.
We do not want to be in the same position in two years as we are today, and that is why this point is extremely important to us.
The second point: The reliability statement for the overall budget pursuant to the graduated control procedure, including the national audit institutions.
It would be desirable if, in the final half-year remaining to the Commission, we could achieve decisive progress here, or at least agree on a common path.
One is expected to be modest as a household auditor.
The third point would be simplification.
We must provide greater support to the lower levels of administration in Member States in implementing EU law.
It becomes apparent that they are completely overwhelmed by large parts of EU law, which is why the error rates are so high.
The fourth point is foreign aid. Here, I would like above all to address Kosovo and the question of cooperation with the United Nations.
The Committee on Budgetary Control has already visited Kosovo, and I would like to announce now that Kosovo will play a certain role in the discharge procedure.
I believe we have so far remained far below the potential of the European Union, and that we urgently need to catch up here.
The fifth point would be improving cooperation among member states in combating fraud.
We have homework primarily for the ladies and gentlemen of the council.

The Parliament will vote on the legal basis, the regulation on combating fraud, in November, and then we will have to jointly consider how we should proceed if we wish to achieve better results in this area.
That has been our wish until now.
Mr. President! India and Europe need more and better contacts with one another at all levels, particularly at the level of so-called ordinary people.
Business travelers, tourists, students, etc. A brief note on this: it also has significance for the Commission's legislative program, which we discussed previously.
In global air traffic, we need effective safety regulations—ones that actually make a difference—not merely symbolic measures serving as mere alibis.
It would therefore be a sensible, truly necessary measure to finally abolish the senseless liquidity regulations and come up with something better.
Mr. President! I voted in favor of the resolution because it is a clear call on the Belarusian government to finally respect human rights.

It is disappointing that the hopes for a democratic development for the people of this last European dictatorship were not fulfilled by the questionable, indeed fraudulent, parliamentary elections of September.
The same applies to the strategic suppression of the opposition and civil society.

At the same time, today we have called on the Council and the Commission to facilitate and intensify contacts between the peoples and to democratize the country by seriously considering the possibility of
Consider reducing visa fees for Belarusian citizens entering the Schengen Area in order to prevent the increasing isolation of Belarus and its citizens.
First and foremost, I wish to protest against the spirit of a statement heard just a few minutes ago, according to which foreigners would flood the labor market.
Secondly, I would have preferred a clearer, more explicit report from the employment committee.
Trust in the social cohesive power of the European Union can only be achieved if fundamental social rights are established as primary European law.

We should send a stronger signal to the Council, the Commission, the Member States, and the ECJ, and not be content with merely demanding a balance between fundamental rights and internal market freedoms, including freedom of movement.
Thus, we are changing nothing. Social rights, like civil liberties, are human rights that must not be restricted by the free movement provisions of the internal market.
The issue is that we must defend and improve the European social model, and it is high time to introduce a social progress clause as a binding protocol to the existing EU treaties.
And it is time to amend the posting of workers directive in such a way that pay and minimum standards requirements may not be limited to minimum requirements.
Mr. President! I would like to join the many colleagues who have already rightly expressed their gratitude to the rapporteur, Doris Pack.
We have someone with her who, hopefully, will not actually shed much blood, but who has invested a great deal of heart and soul into this space.
I share your pleasure, and that of the colleagues who have also said so, that some progress has been made, also in the field of police cooperation.
This is important in order for actual life on the ground to function in reasonable ways.

I still believe, however, that we must continually remind our counterparts in Bosnia and Herzegovina that they themselves must contribute considerably more, indeed more than is currently being done in certain areas.
Internal cooperation, and the willingness to engage in this internal cooperation within ethnic groups, is something we must continually demand.
It's not the case that everyone tries to go their own way.
The integration into regional cooperation in the Western Balkans is also important.
Madam President! The distance between the European Union and its citizens must not become as great as it has become.
We must ensure that it is reduced.
In this context, the ombudsman has an important role to play, particularly when and where he performs it well.
It is particularly important that we can count on the ombudsman's commitment and discretion.
The issue is that we should not raise expectations that no one can fulfill.

But it is also about ensuring that wherever shortcomings have arisen within the administration of European institutions, they are addressed and, where possible, reasonable solutions are sought that can then reliably guarantee satisfaction for the citizen.
The previous practice has shown that the Ombudsman takes on this task seriously and performs it well.
We can hope that this will continue to be the case, and we thank him and the rapporteur for this report.
I'm a bit nervous.
Dear colleagues, ten years after the realization of the monetary union, Europe is facing a serious crisis.
Banks collapse or are propped up with billions from the state budget, sales decline sharply, and millions of people fear for their jobs and their future.
But not only has the market failed, the prevailing politics also seem incapable of learning from their mistakes.
We believe that you do not particularly wish to distinguish yourself through such interim remarks. We believe that serious errors were made in the design of the Economic and Monetary Union.
Such an error is the structural separation of money and fiscal policy. One cannot create a common currency without simultaneously harmonizing tax and spending policies at least to some extent.
I believe the economic imbalances within the eurozone have greatly intensified.
What we actually need now is better coordination of economic and, above all, tax policy.
We need effective measures against tax dumping; furthermore, tax havens must be closed down, and capital flows must finally be brought under control again.
The second major error lies in the design of the Stability and Growth Pact.

Anyone who considers fiscal consolidation unavoidable in times like these evidently lives in a different world. Nothing would be more disastrous than responding to this economic crisis right now with public spending cuts.
The Stability Pact has clearly demonstrated its failure.
It should be replaced by an integrated European strategy for solidarity and sustainable development.
In our view, what is needed is an investment offensive to renew public infrastructure and improve the situation of socially disadvantaged people across Europe.
A third flaw lies in the structure of the European Central Bank itself, which is not subject to democratic oversight and is solely committed to the objective of price stability.

We advocate for democratic oversight of the European Central Bank, and furthermore call for a revision of the ECB's monetary policy mandate, under which growth and employment must be promoted on an equal footing with price stability in the future.
The current crisis also presents an opportunity for profound reforms of the European monetary and financial architecture.
This opportunity should not be recklessly squandered.
Madam President, Commissioner, President of the Council, esteemed colleagues! Indeed, the automotive industry is in crisis, and there are many reasons for this.
In particular, the financial crisis has further exacerbated the crisis in this sector, and currently efforts are underway in the member states to develop solutions.
The European automotive industry is a key industry.
And therefore, we also need European solutions for this key European industry in order to overcome the crisis.
Almost all automobile manufacturers have European structures.

And in order to make necessary investments in environmentally friendly vehicles—something that has been mentioned several times today—so as to be able to pursue environmentally sound and fuel-efficient technologies, automobile manufacturers require public guarantees or low-interest loans from the European Investment Bank.
But the supplier industry—this is very important to me—must not be overlooked in this context.
Especially in the supplier industry, it is small and medium-sized enterprises that create jobs and drive innovation.
But what can the European Union do to protect this key industry?
At the previous plenary session, the Council President made a proposal to protect key industries.
A good proposal. In my opinion, if one consistently carries this proposal through to its conclusion, it means a Volkswagen Act for the entire European automotive industry.
And this would certainly be a useful tool.
But this protection instrument is currently being challenged by the European Commission.
And since my question to the Commission: Will the European Commission stick to its lawsuit despite the crisis in the automotive industry, or will it now change course in light of the crisis?
Mr. President, Mr. President of the European Court of Auditors, Mr. Vice-President of the European Commission, dear colleagues! It has now become customary to observe,
We must recognize that, in the relationship between expenditures at the European level and administration at the national level, a declaration of reliability cannot be given repeatedly.

I recall conversations with the former President of the Court of Auditors, Professor Friedmann, who once told me that, given the existing structures, it is absolutely impossible to issue a declaration of reliability.
Therefore, we should already consider together how this instrument can be imbued with life in such a way that the objective—reliability statement—can be achieved if it is justified.
Here we must clearly distinguish between several things.
First, a budget containing approximately 95 subsidies is far more susceptible to fraud than a national, regional, or local budget.

A subsidy budget, which is largely managed by the member states—precisely those states that, in the new financial period, have demanded significantly greater autonomy from us, as the criticism during the previous funding period had been that too much was centrally controlled.
This naturally also means that responsibility for the funds must be assumed at the regional and national levels.
And I would like to briefly touch upon a third topic area.
We also need to learn how to distinguish between fraud and waste.
These are also things that are always gladly thrown together.
I also get annoyed when EU funds are used to support things that are not absolutely necessary.
But that was not fraud; it was wastefulness.

Therefore, those responsible for the projects, particularly in the field of structural policy, as well as the member states, also bear the responsibility that funds are not wasted and that only projects are supported which truly generate added value for the region.
Therefore, I believe we should certainly consider from time to time shifting parts of structural policy toward interest-free loans.
After all, if member states have to repay, they only support what they actually need. Many thanks!
It is not only me who finds it difficult to join in the growing self-praise by European decision-makers regarding their handling of the financial market crisis.
one would have to ask first about responsibility, as to how all this could have come about.
It simply isn't true that all the problems we currently face constitute a tsunami, something natural and governed by law, but rather that they are man-made.
Given all the regulatory fervor that this union has sparked, one cannot help but ask: Why was no action taken when banks began crossing national borders on a significant scale?
Why was nothing done despite appropriate warnings, as derivatives had so insinuated themselves?
I clearly remember that Nobel laureate Joseph Stiglitz was here and addressed all these points.
There was silence, there was a taboo.
One would have to start there, because recognizing one's own mistakes holds the key to finding solutions for the future.
Mr. President, esteemed ladies and gentlemen, Commissioner! My question concerns your opinion on what you think about Iceland and Hungary introducing the euro as a second currency.
Do you believe this could solve the problem of these two countries?
The second question is: Do you believe it would be possible to introduce a risk premium for derivatives, which would then be used to strengthen bank equity?
My third question is: Do you not believe that a reduction in value-added tax would accordingly support imports, particularly from China and India?
Would it not be more reasonable to consider tax reductions for our businesses and our workers, to provide investment incentives, and to adjust progressive depreciation accordingly?

The central point is that low-value goods are increased by a factor of 10, so that fictitious profits are not taxed, the money remains within the enterprises, and wages can thus be paid more adequately.
Madam President! The comprehensive report highlights how many human rights violations occur around the world every single day.
This is particularly alarming 60 years after the proclamation of the Charter of Human Rights and calls upon us to intensify our efforts even further.
For this reason as well, I would like to advocate for establishing the current Subcommittee on Human Rights as a main committee.
Today we have awarded the Sakharov Prize to Hu Jia, who advocates for human rights and the environment in China.
However, as he is incarcerated, he was unable to take him up in person.
Shame on China, and tomorrow there will be a debate here on human rights violations in Russia.
In our relations with these two countries, we must ensure that human rights, above all else, are given a higher priority than economic interests.
We demand freedom for Hudzhia in China and freedom for Mikhail Khodorkovsky in Russia.

Mr. President, esteemed members of the Council, Commissioner, dear colleagues! As rapporteur for the Anti-Fraud Office, one makes interesting observations—such as the fact that everyone talks about combating fraud, yet nobody actually does anything.
Or the experience of member states carefully calculating their payments to the European Union with a fine pencil, yet pulling on tight-fitting trousers when it comes to spending this money?
and even make it easier for fraudsters, for example, by the fact that it still takes an average of 36 months before irregularities are reported to the European Union at all.
The European Parliament does not agree with this.
We want an efficient fraud prevention strategy that also includes prevention.
We want the member states to make fraud prevention a priority and for us to discuss together the needs and challenges involved.
We aim to foster greater interest among national judicial authorities in further pursuing OLAF's findings.
With crooked routes, there must no longer be areas left without legal protection, as has been the case until now.
We need to achieve parity between European money and national money.
We want an independent authority that, in fulfilling its duties, has the necessary resources and legal basis.
And we want an agency that can cooperate silently and efficiently with the member states.
Our response to legal basis 1073 covers all these points.
We advocate further developing this regulation as the core of the Anti-Fraud Agency together with the Council.
We call on the Council to incorporate our comments, we call on the Council to begin discussions on the further development of this regulation.
We intend to bring the issues concerning OLAF to a resolution.
I thank all factions of the House for supporting this in a resolution that we will vote on tomorrow.
I appeal to the French and Czech Presidencies of the Council to pick up the ball thrown by Parliament and pass it back.
We have also incorporated solutions in our proposals that were developed and submitted by the Council's working group.
On our part, I would like to express our interest in a swift conclusion of this regulation and assure you of our constructive willingness to discuss the matter based on our proposals.
The consolidation of all OLAF legal bases, as the Council has indeed envisaged, would entail an additional substantial loss of time with an uncertain outcome.
OLAF will soon celebrate his tenth birthday.
A consolidation means another ten years without a strengthened legal basis.
We take the fight against fraud far too seriously for that.
That is why we want to implement what is feasible now, in cooperation with the Council.
Many thanks!
Mr. President! I would like to take up a remark made earlier by my Austrian colleague, Paul Rübig.

We spend less than 1% of the European Union's gross domestic product on Europe, while simultaneously demanding that the European Union take on ever more tasks for an ever-increasing number of member states.

This calculation cannot add up; it is therefore genuinely unbelievable that we have been pursuing a strict austerity policy for many years, yet the austerity policy is not only maintained through frugality
not to use our funds, but essentially to enable finance ministers to collect money again by the end of the year and have it flow back into their national budgets.
We would have enough new tasks for which we should spend our money.
We also have tasks that we have not adequately funded financially for years.
I believe the EU's information budgets should be significantly increased if we truly want to engage European citizens with Europe.
And there are numerous tasks in the field of student and apprentice exchange where Europe could play a significant role.
The Commission is advancing into a traditionally state-reserved domain within the justice sector.
This reflects the changed living conditions of people in our Union.
But and this remark has already been made several times: integration must not extend too far into traditionally developed structures, particularly those that are functioning effectively.
Not everything may be swept under the rug.
Public documents and public notaries are interpreted quite differently in many member states.
In Austria, but not only here, the notary public has long been established as a public authenticating authority and enjoys a high level of security and trust.
This must not be put at risk unnecessarily, merely because such occupational groups do not exist elsewhere.
Therefore, integration and mutual openness, yes, but with moderation.
Madam President, esteemed colleagues! Plant protection products have been and remain essential tools in agricultural production.
Farmers need a minimum selection of plant protection products, as this concerns the protection of the domestic cultural landscape, high-quality nutrition, and the preservation of resources.
We often conduct discussions about chemicals on a highly irrational level.
To obtain reasonable results, however, we must think and act rationally.
The consequences of the original parliamentary stance would have been fatal: a broad ban on plant protection products, resulting in lower agricultural yields and, ultimately, rising food prices.
Therefore, I am glad that a barely acceptable compromise was reached in the trilogue negotiations.
It is welcome that we have maintained the Three-Zone Regulation, albeit with significant exceptions for the member states.

A courageous step toward a consistent internal market solution would look different at this juncture. Rather than anticipating a final definition of endocrine-disrupting substances, we should uphold acceptable compromises and genuinely grant the European Commission the four years needed to present a scientifically sound definition.
Otherwise, we would be stretching the precautionary principle beyond its appropriate scope at this point.
Lastly, I find it regrettable that we do not go further and initiate a follow-up assessment of the agreement now reached.
Mr. President! The last time similar statements were made during the visit of a head of government or head of state, the President of this House intervened forcefully.
At that time, it concerned an issue that was personally important to him, namely the Lisbon Treaty.
If today the president, the democratically elected president of a country, speaks and is severely interrupted and disturbed by representatives from the socialist side, the president does not shout.
Do you know what this is?
This is not democracy; it is arbitrariness, and I demand that appropriate measures be taken.
Madam President! Children and politics—rarely do the interests of citizens and politicians diverge so greatly.
Across Europe, the issue of children ranks highest among citizens.
For politicians, children arrange concerns about distant lives, as we can again observe here.
I know of no country that has a dedicated children's ministry. Likewise, within the Commission there is no specific representative for the interests of our youngest citizens.
In parliament, the issue of children is scattered across all committees.
Therefore, I would like to particularly highlight at this point that the Commission is addressing childcare facilities and the Barcelona targets today.
Mr. President! In our parliamentary working group on the Quality of Childhood, we have observed the following: Our children live in a very complex world.
The future of a person who is a child here, now, and today is completely uncertain.
A child today can climb all the way to the top of the social ladder or fall all the way to the bottom.
It can remain within its own cultural context or seek a new one.
It may follow a similar life path as its parents or do exactly the opposite.
One may marry someone of the other gender or one's own gender.
All of this was completely different more than 50 years ago, when we brought the united Europe into the world.
The diversity that distinguishes us Europeans has become a defining factor in the lives of children.
The concept of diversity must therefore be incorporated into the pedagogy of childcare workers and teachers.
This is currently taken into account far too little.
European childcare facilities and schools are struggling with the challenge of integration.
In matters of integration, we must press the reset button and go back to the beginning.
Therefore, we approach this topic once again entirely without prejudice, in keeping with the motto Unity through Diversity.

And furthermore, I urge all European Parliament members who follow me not only to advocate for gender mainstreaming, but also to fight for child mainstreaming, especially at this time of global financial crisis, because otherwise we will burden our children with enormous debts.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. I will keep this brief.
I would like to sincerely thank you for these constructive contributions to the debate that I have heard here.
I would like to convey just two messages before concluding. Firstly, I expressly thank the Commissioner for not opposing the concept of minimum requirements of the safety net in principle.
This proposal is no devil's work, and the other one is no bureaucratic monster either.
It is a tool for problem-solving, one to which we should give a chance.
Therefore, I would simply like to advocate for it here.
And the second thing I would like to say, which is now directed at my British friends, and indeed, across all the parties.
My dear British friends, I do understand Caroline, I do understand your problem.
I can well understand that, for a certain period of time, a country might prioritize energy security over compliance with an air pollutant threshold.
I have great understanding for that.
I am the last person who would deny Your Majesty additional time when it comes to building new coal-fired power plants.
We could talk about it, couldn't we?
The problem is, we did exactly that not.
This conversation has never taken place during the past four months.
I would like to ask you: this is now the outlook in the second reading; at this point, please be open-minded once again and say, Let us discuss a compromise here.

I am in favor of abandoning what, in my view, is a completely irrational resistance—unjustifiable from a competition policy and environmental policy perspective—that represents a fundamental opposition to minimum standards.
The crucial point is where a compromise can be reached, which I hope will ultimately be achieved in a second reading.
Incidentally, I do not believe we should refrain from seeking a compromise during the first reading.
This legislation is too complex for that purpose.

I do not wish to conclude this legislative term as rapporteur and leave the next Parliament with legislation in which a legal text is being discussed that is inconsistent, illogical, and contradictory.
Therefore, I kindly request your support for the compromises when we vote here in one hour.
Mr. President! Dear colleagues! I believe the crucial question regarding all the economic stimulus programmes currently being developed across Europe is, of course, who ultimately receives the money?
additional blank cheques will continue to be issued to the banks, even though swift nationalization would ultimately be considerably cheaper for the taxpayer.
Should corporations and high-income earners be further relieved, despite having been fattened for years across Europe through tax giveaways?

The more money is squandered on such purposes, the more inevitable it becomes that these programs will fail, and the more likely it is that the European economy will sink further and further into a very dangerous downward spiral.

The years-long policy of privatization, deregulation, and liberalization—precisely this policy—has concentrated incomes ever more heavily among the top ten thousand, and it is this policy that is responsible for the current crisis in which we find ourselves today.
Anyone who believes he can overcome this crisis by simply continuing exactly this policy with minor adjustments has completely failed to understand the situation.
The exact opposite of this is necessary.
Instead of purchasing bad paper from banks, tax funds should be used for the renovation of schools and hospitals, and for the ecological restructuring of the European economy.

Where private companies receive public funds, it must be established that no public money is provided without employment guarantees, and above all, no public money without the acquisition of public ownership rights, so that the state and, in particular, the general public can also benefit from future earnings.
The best possible economic stimulus program currently available would be a radical redistribution of income and wealth from the top down.
The low-wage sector in Europe must be reduced, rather than continually promoted.
We need higher minimum wages, we need better social benefits in Europe.

And we need taxes that ensure that millionaires and the beneficiaries of the past financial market orgy now also bear the immense losses that have occurred, rather than the vast majority of citizens who never benefited from the financial boom in the first place.
I believe that, at present, the socially just approach is also the only economically sensible one.
It is the only way to break the devastating dynamics of the crisis.

Mr. President! In early March, the political prisoners Mikhail Khodorkovsky and Platon Lebedev were transferred from Chita in Siberia to Moscow to be charged once again on utterly untenable allegations.
The first public hearing day was scheduled for March 31.
On the same day, the human rights consultations between the EU and Russia were supposed to take place.
These important talks have now been postponed by the Russian side indefinitely.
Russia thus clearly demonstrates the low value it places on human rights.
The judiciary continues to be used not only for purposes other than justice, but also to eliminate regime opponents, thereby deceiving the EU.
Mr. President! Mr. Commissioner, thank you very much, dear colleagues! I am particularly grateful to Commissioner Verheugen for once again highlighting the ban on animal testing.

I have drawn the honourable Member's attention to this, as it reminds me that this is now the third amendment to cosmetic legislation that I would have the opportunity to accompany. The sixth amendment, the seventh amendment, and now this amendment to a regulation.
And indeed, we have managed to ban animal experiments.
We have managed it so that, for example, consumers know how long a product remains fresh by seeing a small container with a number inside indicating how long it is still valid.

I say this also to some colleagues, particularly to Ms. Reutow, who unfortunately was not able to join us at the beginning. Labels are never mere warnings; a product must never be considered safe at any point, then it must not be placed on the market, nor may it be marketed.
Every product on this European market must be safe and without risk.
But labeling gives consumers the opportunity to make a choice.
This is democracy, and this is your right to choose.
I believe that we have very good legislation.
I have tried to make the procedure as transparent as possible.
I have tried, as Mrs. Costette also mentioned, to bring together differing opinions.

I am determined that we create legislation that is good for everyone—the consumers in the European Union, the industry that must work with this legislation, and ultimately all those who will benefit from it.
And if I say to colleague Schnellhardt, I'm saying it again: yes, DEO affects everyone differently, but the claim that it keeps you sweat-free still stands.
Therefore, it is important that the statements regarding the depth of my eye folds after one week in Strasbourg are reasonably realistic and reliable.
I would like to once again express my gratitude to the Commissioner, also in regard to the statements he has made, to my fellow colleagues, and to my assistant, who has done the bulk of the work.
Thank you.

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, esteemed colleagues! The report by my colleague Van Nistelrooij on the Green Paper and the future shape of cohesion policy is undoubtedly one of the most significant produced by the Committee on Regional Development in recent years.
We agree with the fundamental concept of the Green Paper, according to which territorial cohesion is indeed about promoting the polycentric development of the EU
ensuring the overall sustainable development of the territorial waters with their various characteristics and features, while simultaneously preserving their diversity.
It is correct that in the upcoming planning period a more comprehensive system of phased assistance for transition regions exceeding the threshold of 75
20 of the protein land product will be exceeded, must be created in order for these regions to obtain a clear status and thus have greater security in their development.
In the report by colleague Crewe on obstacles to the launch of the structural funds, the most significant shortcomings are rightly listed, which applicants face when

In the utilization of structural funds, challenges include, for example, excessive administrative overhead, an overly complex regulatory framework, and slow, cumbersome centralized administration in the member states.
Various recommendations are made to the Commission regarding effective measures that should be taken to overcome these obstacles.
I would simply like to highlight and emphasize two such measures.

First, project managers are currently required to retain their project documentation for a period of ten years in order to have it available for inspections, which may be carried out by the Commission.
This involves excessively bureaucratic effort, particularly for smaller projects.
It is correct that this is now to be reduced to a period of three years. Secondly, the evaluation criteria of the European Commission for innovative projects are causing significant problems.
For innovative projects, the same evaluation criteria must not apply as for other projects.
It is simply necessary that something different takes place here.

Madam President! If Europe takes money into its hands and, above all, if the Member States take a great deal of money into their hands to assist the automotive industry in its current crisis, then the objective cannot merely be to secure the status quo and prevent failures; rather, it involves a range of other concerns.
The most important ones have been mentioned.
The challenge is to seek employment for people, and to secure this employment for them in a sustainable, long-term manner.
And for this purpose, it is necessary that we promote new technologies, innovation, and above all sustainability within the transportation system to a greater extent than we have done so far.

We should therefore link all our measures to these objectives, so that we do not have to accept criticism in a few years' time. We should have thought about all this many years ago; then we would not be facing the next and new crisis.
Madam President! It would be kind of you to grant me the additional half-minute that my fellow Green, Frau Breyer, has just received.
The Commission had submitted a sound proposal for updating the regulation on novel foods.
Unfortunately, some amendments attempt to supplement this proposal with provisions that are incompatible with the objectives of the regulation or even seek to interfere with existing case law.
The obligation of food business operators to monitor novel foods with regard to human health and animal welfare would simply overwhelm traders.
And yet again, there is an attempt to circumvent existing GMO regulations, namely the demand for special labeling of such novel foods derived from animals fed with genetically modified feed.
We have heard it. I think:
Fundamentally, anything relating to GMO legislation should not be addressed in the Noble Food Regulation.
I say this even though I myself, for example, had submitted a proposal to specifically exclude certain new plant varieties from the definition of novel foods.
But it cannot be tolerated that interested parties within this house misuse the Nobil Food Regulation for electoral campaign purposes.
It appears that the same applies to amendments 62 and 90, for which the Greens have requested a roll-call vote.
Who, dear colleagues, do you actually want to put on public display with this?
My political group has always advocated for the consumer's right to know what is contained in foodstuffs.
Why then should one not indicate a nano origin in the ingredient declaration?
And although Amendment 62 attempts rather clumsily to anticipate my report on food labelling, I must tell you that I consider the labelling by Noble Food to be quite compatible with my report.

I therefore recommend my parliamentary group's approval of the aforementioned proposals, and I will include references in my report on the food labelling regulation to the portion of the Noble Food Regulation dealing with labelling.
This is possible because we postponed the first reading on the Food Labeling directive to the next legislative term, against the wishes of the Greens.
But now it has become clear to everyone how advantageous this postponement was.
Briefly on cloning—it is animal cruelty.
We are against it.
Nevertheless, foodstuffs must be incorporated into this regulation accordingly, as otherwise they would fall outside the scope of
Madam President, it's very simple.
Anyone who does not wish this man to preside over the ceremonial opening session of the European Parliament should support my amendment to the rules of procedure.

Madam President, Madam President, esteemed Commissioner, dear colleagues! One thing is clear from the outset: the provision of food to those in need, particularly during these economically challenging times, is not up for debate in the upcoming vote on our colleague Tschikersky's report.
Therefore, I would like to make clear right at the beginning that it was not easy to find a common line within our parliamentary group.
Therefore, I respect each parliamentary colleague's personal decision to deviate from the intended rejection of the report and not to support it.
Why cannot I accept the outcome of the committee vote in this form?
There are a number of reasons for this, none of which have anything at all—absolutely nothing—to do with assistance for the needy within the European Union during these economically difficult times.

On the contrary, the amendment proposals submitted by my colleague Nils Busk on behalf of the ALDE Group, though regrettably deemed inadmissible, were intended to place the existing system on a sustainable foundation.
For the existing system is an anachronism from times of agricultural overproduction, which thankfully belongs to the past.

Madam President, ladies and gentlemen! A microfinancing instrument for the unemployed, those threatened by unemployment, and particularly for people who do not have access to conventional credit markets, is a good intention, a commendable goal, and is generally supported by our parliamentary group.
However, the performance demonstrated by the Commission and the Council so far in this procedure is simply inadequate, partly incorrect, and cannot be supported by our parliamentary group.
First point of criticism: the issue of financial resources.

What lies on the table in terms of financial resources is not a financial allocation for a program to combat unemployment. This cannot be described as a microfinance program; at best, it is nanofinance.
Secondly, we are fundamentally opposed to such sleight-of-hand maneuvers that have no real effect, come at the expense of other programs, and thus dissipate endlessly.
Thirdly, the view that sustainability and success of such a program must organically include mentoring and coaching.
Many new start-ups, particularly in the microfinance sector, fail; therefore, provision must also be made within the program to address this.
Fourthly, it must be ensured that social security benefits in the Member States are not withdrawn for the purpose of accessing this programme, otherwise it will be ineffective.
Thus, unemployment cannot be effectively combated in a sustainable manner.
We will reject it in this form.
Mr. President! Today, after eight years, we all essentially know that the previous Afghanistan strategy, based on military means, has failed.
The imbalance between military operations on one hand and insufficient resources for civilian purposes on the other effectively prevents any improvement in living conditions in Afghanistan.
And that is why our most important message as UNGL is that a radical change in strategy is urgently needed.
We need a radical change in strategy, away from military approaches and especially toward people.
This means that 40 of the Afghans are unemployed. More than half of them live in extreme poverty, lacking access to healthcare and adequate education.
The social issue must be given priority, and this is what we expect from the Council, the Commission, and all parties involved.
This naturally includes strengthening responsible governance, bolstering agriculture, and integrating former Taliban fighters.
But I tell you quite honestly: anyone who only travels halfway down this path and then returns to military adventures misses every opportunity.
It is five minutes to twelve.

Mr. President! The current debate is a central discussion about the nature of the European Union.
The question is: market or social policy?
The Commission's current considerations regarding the future of cohesion policy are unacceptable.
The structural funds must be strengthened, not reduced.
Europe must not do less, but rather more, to overcome economic and social development disparities.

I say this quite openly: The renationalization, even of only parts of today's cohesion policy, would have serious consequences for disadvantaged regions, both in Germany and beyond, for example in eastern Germany.
After the most recent round of enlargement of the European Union, the differences between regions within the Union have indeed grown.
Moreover, I would like to emphasize that socio-economic conditions in regions just above the 75 threshold of GDP per capita should also be recognized.
An abrupt cessation of funding after 2013 would be devastating for these regions.
Ladies and gentlemen! Of course, strengthening the specific urban development funding, city-oriented funding provided by the European Union, is extremely important.
This is important because significant economic and financial potential exists there, and because the crisis has particularly affected this region.
One final word about border regions.
As an eastern German member of parliament, I tell you that there we must invest a great deal, do a great deal.
And there I have serious concerns that the Commission will fail.

Mr. President! Regional structural funding is a key instrument in European integration, and in the past, Target 2 funding has been perceived by people in many regions as a driving force behind local structural and economic development.
It is an important element for managing structural change in regions and also in municipalities.
Here, Europe is truly experienced by citizens on the ground, because it also has an impact right there.
We simply need simpler, clearer, and more transparent structures in the cohesion funds, along with improved controls, to ensure that funds are used sustainably and efficiently.
Cohesion, the convergence in social and economic development, is a key objective of the EU, and therefore we must not weaken this instrument in the future, but rather strengthen it.
European cohesion policy needs greater clarity, greater transparency, efficiency, and also the individual responsibility of each member state.
And therefore, it is important to remain with the system of additive funding.
We do not, however, wish for a renationalization of structural funding.
Therefore, I would like to request that the Commission ensure the funding criteria from 2013 onwards meet these requirements.

Madam President! It seems to me that, for us—meaning the EU as a whole, but particularly for Parliament—it is somewhat symptomatic that we reflexively engage in discussions about situations in countries whose circumstances are by no means new.
It is unfortunately often the case that events occurring overnight lead us to suddenly pour forth in waves of political demands, some of which I find somewhat questionable.
I believe it does not reflect well on us to allow a failed airplane attack alone to trigger a general debate about Yemen.
I believe we should be clear about the fact that it would be to our advantage to devise a strategy for dealing with this situation.

I also believe that the situation in Yemen requires careful analysis, specifically the fact that it is a failed state in which the government no longer controls large parts of the country, and we should examine for ourselves what risks for Europe arise from this.
The risks appear to be that terrorists are being trained there, and the risks also seem to be emerging along the coast of Yemen, where piracy is evidently taking place.
Therefore, it is up to us—and perhaps also to Ms. Ashton—to consider what we can do about these risks.
In my view, the answer primarily lies in how we can strengthen the government in Yemen, enable it to regain control over the country, and thereby contain these risks.

All other discussions about the long-term development of the country must of course also take place, but I do not believe it is truly meaningful or that we would be taken seriously if we were to now present a list of demands spanning all policy areas—from media freedom to women's rights—here in parliament.
I believe this will not achieve the desired outcome in Yemen; instead, we should focus on what specific assistance we can realistically provide in the current situation to address an urgent problem.

Mr. President! Distinguished ladies and gentlemen! The massacre on November 23 of last year in Maguindanao Province in the Philippines was a black day for human rights and a barbaric act of terrorism.
57 dead, raped women, injured, they speak a bloody language.
As a journalist, I particularly note that among the dead were 30 journalists.
According to the renowned International Crisis Group, in no documented case have more journalists ever been murdered at once.
For this reason, it is appropriate for the European Parliament to use clear language, as we are doing today in the draft resolution.

However, as the EPP, we call for softening the current text at three points by means of separate elections, because we should support the Philippine government in its fight against terrorism and violence, and therefore refrain from overly accusatory remarks directed at the government.
Therefore, I request, on behalf of the EPP, that Recital F be deleted. This recital accuses the Philippines of exhibiting all signs of a non-functioning legal system.
Secondly, quotation: In point two, we would like to remove the assumption of so-called initial delays in clarification.
Finally, point 6 claims that there are missing persons who are imprisoned in Philippine detention facilities.
This is also currently an unsubstantiated assumption and, in our opinion, should be removed.

Madam President! The issue here is that we, as the European Union, must pursue a clear strategy and prevent a gold or oil rush.

Here, we need with our partners in the Arctic mutually acceptable and reliable agreements.
We need cooperation instead of confrontation.
We need dialogue in the field of environmental protection to preserve natural resources and nature.

to counter job losses, thirdly to allocate public investments toward job creation and sustainable ecological growth, and fourthly to strengthen and expand social safety nets and ensure access.

If individual nation states have a vital interest in taking security measures beyond this, I likewise consider them liable for costs in this area.
Who entrusts the security of the sovereign domain, pays for it.
I see it differently, certainly, in technical or personal matters, which may inevitably result in additional costs for the passenger, provided that it is clearly disclosed what is involved.
Notably, however, within the scope of the impact assessment presented to us by the Commission, an amount of approximately 0.1 of the total transport subsidy is allocated for these measures.
We should therefore not start too high.
I believe we can reach a viable compromise with the Federal Republic of Germany, which has exceptional reservations in the area of subsidiarity.

to reconcile serious crime prevention with the fundamental rights of every individual to privacy protection and also informational self-determination.

And for me, it is actually no question at all that we must firmly integrate controls, appeals, access rights, claims for compensation, and the duration of data storage into the agreement.

And this would, in my view, also be a contribution to protecting the personality rights of the individuals concerned.
I believe it is positive that we postponed the decision on the passenger data agreements under discussion, in order to establish a foundational model for all future agreements of this kind.
We must also develop, and provide the Commission with, a negotiating framework that duly takes into account our own views on data protection.

Mr. President! We have rightly pointed out the preambles concerning the importance of the EIB.

Policy coherence in the interest of development means that we do not take back with one hand what we give with the other.

It makes little sense, for example, to channel development aid into building up agriculture in developing countries while simultaneously undermining local markets through direct or indirect export subsidies.

Likewise, no matter how vigorously we advocate for better healthcare, we undermine our efforts if we hinder the trade of generic drugs or drive medication prices to unaffordable levels through patent extensions.

And agrofuels may slightly reduce CO2 emissions in the EU, but they cause deforestation and land-use changes in developing countries, thereby increasing climate change—precisely what we sought to avoid—let alone the displacement of indigenous populations, loss of biodiversity, and reduction of arable land for food production.
Currently, we—meaning the EU as such—are not particularly cohesive.
That policy measures should not contradict one another has been recognized by the EU in theory for some time.
The Treaty of Lisbon enshrines policy coherence for development.
Article 208 states: In implementing political measures that may affect developing countries, the Union shall take into account the objectives of development cooperation.
We must measure ourselves against this requirement.
This year, we are reviewing progress toward achieving the Millennium Development Goals.
It is clear that without political coherence, we will not be able to fulfill our promises.

We must become aware of this responsibility, all of us who enact and implement laws. Before we adopt any measure, there must be an impact assessment, so that we can actually identify what negative effects European legislation might have on developing countries.
We need expertise in the Council, the Commission, and the Parliament that can identify inconsistencies.
The Commission's work programme on policy coherence represents a positive step forward.
And now it depends on how this plan is implemented.

Policy coherence in the interest of development means that, in the context of fisheries reform, we must take greater account of the interests of local people and must not subordinate them to the interests of European fishing corporations.
It means that we must dry up tax havens into which funds from developing countries flow.
It means that we cannot support ACTA if there remains concern that this agreement could disrupt access to medicines or technologies in developing countries.
To date, the Commission has not been able to dispel this suspicion.

Policy coherence also means that, in establishing the External Service, development policy must remain a strong, autonomous policy field, and the competences of the Commissioner for Development must be expanded, not reduced.
It also entails enabling fair trade in public procurement by municipalities, as my colleague Heide Rühle has proposed.
In many cases, we Members of Parliament must closely monitor the Commission and the Council to ensure they develop coherent policies.
In many cases, however, it also lies with us members of parliament to be coherent in the interest of development.
We need to coordinate more effectively among the committees, and we require a permanent rapporteur.
The report, which the Development Committee unanimously adopted, makes numerous sound recommendations and moves us a significant step forward.
I would like to thank all shadow rapporteurs, the Ombudsman, and all the NGOs for their support and collaboration, and I hope that our joint report can also be adopted by the plenary tomorrow.

Madam President, dear colleagues! For months now, we have been discussing the issue of driving and rest periods in road transport.
And I wonder what arguments will now be marshaled to prove that everything we have been discussing for months is nonsense.
The most recent argument is that we must urgently change things here, so that nothing changes.
Do you not realize, dear Frau Bauer, how absurd this argumentation has become, how thoroughly it is permeated by lies and half-truths?
This debate is nothing other than about extending the working hours of self-employed individuals.
In labour time law, however, the issue for the past 150 years has essentially been the protection of people against excessively long working hours.
This applies both to self-employed individuals and to employed workers.
It makes no difference whatsoever whether it is a self-employed individual who is overburdened, or an employer who overburdens an employed driver.
The aim is to protect people from themselves and from excessively long working hours, and in some cases also from employers and clients.
Secondly, in the matter of road traffic safety, it is claimed here that nothing whatsoever could possibly happen.
Ladies and gentlemen, we are not in a DIY hardware store and we are not amateur craftsmen. We cannot legislate according to the principle: Let's just see what comes out of it.
After all, everyone knows that excessively long working hours are designed to increase the risk of accidents, particularly in road traffic, with serious consequences.
Third point: Who exactly do you envision as being able to monitor such a highly differentiated regulation on our roads in the future?
Today, a state labor court cannot determine whether someone is ostensibly self-employed or truly self-employed without extensive document review.
Stop this nonsense; it's about fair competition.
The issue is not about establishing a precedent today or tomorrow for future discussions on extending working hours.
Don't play games with this house. Thank you.
Madam President! Today, as for many years now, I am advocating for road safety, improved working conditions for our professional drivers, and the enhancement of their occupational status.

And clearly, today it is not about the traffic safety-related regulations concerning driving and rest periods, which all self-employed and employed professional drivers must comply with; today it is about working time regulation.
It is primarily a workers' protection regulation, serving solely the social protection of drivers and by no means the safety of traffic.
We don't need to protect self-employed individuals from themselves.
Compliance with working hours is neither verifiable nor is there a justifiable reason for monitoring among self-employed truck and bus drivers, without imposing significant bureaucratic burden.
Limiting self-employed professional firefighters—such as self-employed craftsmen, architects, or members of parliament in the future—to their permissible working hours is the wrong signal.

fought by drivers.

Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen! Only a few days ago, we watched in dismay on television as vast regions of Poland and other Central European countries were submerged by floodwaters.
People have died in the floods, and our deepest sympathy goes out to their families. Villages, towns, agricultural land, and infrastructure have been destroyed.

In dealing with damage of this magnitude, these countries should also experience the solidarity of the member states of the European Union.
Following the flood disaster of 1997, Poland and, this time also other Central European states, have again been affected by a flood catastrophe.
We still cannot regulate the weather, and flooding is characteristic of rivers at certain times of year.
But the questions we must ask are whether, being aware that such natural events can always occur, the appropriate measures for prevention against
whether damages caused by such natural events occurred and whether, for example, the issues of sustainable disaster protection received appropriate consideration in landscape planning.
When assistance is provided, it should first and foremost benefit those directly affected by the natural disaster, in order to secure their livelihood.

Yet, international cooperation should also be strengthened, since natural disasters do not respect national borders, and more coordinated efforts at the European level should be made by member states to prevent damage caused by natural disasters.

That jointly implemented precautionary measures can provide protection is demonstrated by the flood protection along the Oder River, which has so far been developed jointly by Polish and German authorities.
But we are by no means at the end of it yet.
We do not actually need a European disaster protection system.
Protecting its country from disasters should primarily be the responsibility of a nation's government, including the development of disaster preparedness plans.

However, the coordination of assistance measures in the event of damage or incidents should also benefit from efficient European coordination, and those organizations that provide aid swiftly and without excessive bureaucracy on a voluntary basis should not be left to bear the costs themselves.

Mr. President! First of all, my heartfelt thanks to Matthieu Grosch for this in-depth report and the immense amount of work he has put into it.

An Europe without dividing borders is inconceivable without a functioning transportation system, and the European economy would be neither successful nor even imaginable without a capable transportation system—without efficient, future-proof systems on land, at sea, and in the air.
In particular, when it comes to environmentally friendly rail transport, we need greater solidarity and less nationalistic self-interest.
For a sustainable future of transportation, all modes of transport must confront the challenges facing society in protecting the environment and in the economy.
To this, the report provides valuable and guiding insights.
I can, however, not agree with the report on one point only. I cannot support establishing a transport fund from the resources of the cohesion policy.

Traffic is undoubtedly a particular carrier of cohesion in Europe. However, a dedicated fund drawn from cohesion policy resources would not fulfill the expectations it raises.

The most important thing citizens want is for decisions to be effectively made and problems to be resolved.

And this is objectively the case: in the event of emergency measures, central regulation and decision-making at the European level become necessary whenever coordination among the member states is insufficient and fails to function.
This lies in line with the decisions of the G20, the G8 of heads of state and government, unfortunately not at the level of the Council.
The council makes him small, small, little card.
This is not an acceptable policy for citizens.
We expect the Council to take action.

Dear Commissioners, dear colleagues! One sometimes wonders what exactly we have been doing over the past year, with all the discussions and debates, and the numerous meetings in the trilogue—situations I could have imagined spending in far more pleasant ways.

And now, over a year after the European elections, when we still proclaimed in the streets and squares our intention to bring the financial markets under control, we are in fact facing a very meager, if not entirely nonexistent, result.
And the problem has already been described.
We are dealing with markets that operate globally today.
The member states still believe they are capable of enforcing these rules through their national supervisory structures.
And precisely that it did not work has also not triggered the financial crisis through a single contribution, but it did contribute to the crisis also affecting Europe.

And therefore, I must already ask myself when this insight, which is so readily expressed in Sunday speeches, will finally be translated into political action—when suddenly the focus shifts to protecting stock exchanges, safeguarding banking structures, and no longer protecting citizens.

We have dedicated ourselves to this task as the European Parliament, and I hope that the Member States will also commit to it, as they share responsibility for implementing proper supervisory rules for citizens, ensuring that individuals' savings remain secure in the long term.

Of course, gentlemen Commissioners, I sincerely hope that the Commission will now genuinely do everything possible to enable us to reach a positive outcome as quickly as possible, in the interest of the people of Europe.
Mr. President! Dear colleagues! In this European Parliament, we are responsible for robust regulation of the financial markets.
All that we have proposed was significantly diluted in the Trialogens.
Council and government do not wish to have strong intervention powers.
Giegold is right when he describes what is currently happening as "church tower thinking."
Rather than strict regulation, the intention appears to be merely constructing the system a little less prone to failures.
We will end up with a fragmented supervisory structure that will be unable to exercise genuine control over financial markets.
Insufficient consequences are drawn from this financial crisis, from this economic crisis, thus preparing the ground for the next crisis.
Many national economies, including in Europe, will not survive the next blows, with all the social and economic consequences for people in Europe.
But you expect that this predatory finance capitalism will be recaptured and the primacy of politics restored.
When UniCredit Bank CEO Alessandro Profumo articulated over the weekend, "We need a strong and unified European financial supervision and a common rule for"
If he advocates stricter capital requirements in the member states, then this is an appeal to this Parliament to actually establish these rules.
It is the responsibility of this European Parliament to exert pressure on the Council to urgently implement effective regulation, which would merely be the first step toward a status quo
Finally, let actions follow the grand promises. My faction stands for strong European regulation.
Anything else would be a danger to the euro, to a united EU, and to democracy.
A poor timing for this hearing.
I would have liked to see the Dutch team win once more, even if it were only for the last time in this World Cup.
Probably a bad omen, since now we're not allowed to watch anymore, and then they'll probably lose after all.

But regarding the matter, Commissioner, you are giving the correct signal. Commissioner, Mr. President, in fact, we are concluding today or tomorrow a legislative package on which we have been discussing for more than two years.
This is not solely due to the controversy, but also to an intervening European election and the entry into force of a new EU treaty, which has required us to make certain adjustments.

But I believe that if we draw a conclusion from this debate today, I arrive at the conclusion that we are now voting on a compromise that is certainly worth supporting, even if one does so with a clenched fist in one's pocket at certain points.
I would like to highlight two aspects.
One aspect is the environmental requirements placed on industrial plants.
The issue here is that we need to implement best available technology in Europe more effectively.
I believe we have found an exceptional provision in the compromise now on the table, which provides the European Commission, Mr. Potočnik, and indeed clearly hands you the necessary tool.

In the coming years, we must pay greater and better attention than we have in the past to ensure that the approximately 52 industrial plants concerned in Europe ultimately employ the best available technology more effectively than they do today.
I have heard many arguments in this debate.
One thing I would like to pick up on and also reflect here, as I simply cannot comprehend it at all.
I have often been told that the use of best available technology is too expensive, and that this jeopardizes the competitiveness of European industry, especially during the crisis.
I would rather not recall my past, but I come from the former GDR, from East Germany.
The economic and ecological collapse of the GDR was also due to having slept through decades of investment in state-of-the-art technology.

I believe that every industry, every industrial enterprise, is obliged to invest in the latest technological standards in order to ensure its existence, as otherwise it will lose its competitiveness and, as a natural byproduct, also reduce pollutant emissions.
I believe we have the opportunity today to enact a law that improves this initial situation.
The second part of the directive—and this is a bit of the clenched fist I mentioned—are large combustion plants.

Once again, as we did in the previous directive, we are discussing temporary exceptions until 2020, partly until 2023, for large combustion plants that are not required to meet environmental requirements, in particular air pollutant limit values.
I am directing this message to the Council, perhaps also a little to all of us, that we really need to carefully consider how meaningful it actually is that we regularly

We welcome ambitious environmental targets, and we are open to discussing them here, but whenever it comes to actually implementing them, translating them into concrete legislation, a tragedy unfolds across Europe.
Then it suddenly becomes clear to the member states what this entails in terms of cost, what bills must be paid, and typically those who advanced funds are then punished.
After all, there are several member states that already meet the requirements today and who will be left in the lurch at the end.
I say this again here: When it comes to industrial plants and large combustion plants, we are essentially talking about coal-fired power stations—this is not science fiction.
We are not talking about things that are not available, but rather about the latest state of the art.
What is available on the market from a competitive and cost perspective can be integrated into these facilities. Against this background, we conclude
Today I abhor a package of legislation which I believe is better than what is currently in force, and therefore I encourage all of us to support it.

I also thank my colleagues who, over the past two years, have contributed to this process, often in a very intense manner, for their support, their cooperation, and above all for the fact that nearly all major factions have signed on to this compromise.
Mr. President! Three interesting things have happened while we have been debating. First of all, it is clear that Europe will become football world champion.
A good omen, I believe.
Who should work, who has to go in?
I don't want to express any preferences at all right now.
Secondly, Germany receives compliments from the UK.
Chris Davis, thank you very much. This doesn't happen every day.
It is worthwhile to keep this in mind.
And thirdly, the world is still in order. Even among the Greens, football is more important than environmental protection.
For unfortunately, our colleague Bas Eikud has already left again.
But of course, heartfelt congratulations to our Dutch friends.
I would like to mention two points regarding the debate I have just been following.
First, I understand the worries and hardships of my Eastern European friends only too well.
After all, as an East German, I'm also half a Central/East European.
I understand that it is not possible to immediately catch up in Eastern Europe with every environmental policy step carried out in Western European countries over the past decades.
Environmental protection at a high level must be affordable.
Yes, that is correct.

But I believe that even if one takes this statement into account, it is still worthwhile to accept this compromise, as it does justice to the differing starting situations in which many member states find themselves.
And secondly, the argument that the use of best available techniques jeopardizes competitiveness, particularly in comparison to China. This argument is not only incorrect, it is also dangerous.
We must come to terms with the fact that industrial processes in Asia are inevitably cheaper than ours.
And we will succeed in this competition only if we employ the best available technology in as many industrial sectors as possible.
Those who do not do so lose their competitiveness.
No one demands that this law require shutting down an industrial facility tomorrow.
No one demands that emissions limits be met overnight, from zero to one hundred, within a week.
We have sufficient transition periods, but it is incorrect to claim that one should forego this for competitive reasons.
I believe we have not only set an environmental policy course with this directive, but also an industrial policy direction, one that is worth working toward in the coming years.

Mr. President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen! I would like to begin by thanking the colleagues from the other political groups for their contributions to the preparation of my report on Kosovo, as well as Frau Eva Marr, who has carried out very valuable work in the secretariat here.

And to you, Mr. Commissioner, I would also like to express my thanks for the excellent cooperation, as well as for what you mentioned—that it was important to you to travel to the northern part of Kosovo, to spend a night there, and thereby clearly demonstrate to the local population that you stand with them as well.
You delivered a speech at the university, inaugurating the EU House there.

I believe this was an important signal to clarify what this European Parliament has consistently expressed in the past and is reaffirming once again: support for an independent Kosovo on its path toward the European Union.
We are currently facing a situation in which, once again, the Kosovo has reached a so-called crucial crossroads for its citizens, particularly members of all ethnic groups in Kosovo.
In a few weeks, the International Court of Justice will deliver its ruling, stating its position on the declaration of independence, which was made slightly over two years ago.

I hope that this decision, this opinion, will then provide an opportunity, so to speak, an open window, as it is called in English, to place regional cooperation on a new foundation, but based on self-determination.

I assume that Kosovo is committed to independence, and it must be made clear that the shared future for all states in the region—Serbia, Kosovo, and all others—lies within the European Union, and that the challenges still remaining on the path toward this goal must also be addressed.
It is also the text of a resolution stating clearly that the future of Kosovo lies within the European Union.
