Madam President, esteemed High Representative, dear Commissioner, dear colleagues! The recent events in Kyrgyzstan, the outbreaks of violence, the massacre of people, the
The burning of houses and the streams of refugees fleeing their homes to escape violence have deeply shaken me and evoked profound sympathy for the victims.
At the same time, these events in Kyrgyzstan once again shed light on the situation in Central Asia, which must not leave us in the European Parliament unmoved.
On the contrary, we should direct our full attention to the countries of this region.

We should not focus solely on those states that are rich in natural resources and of particular economic interest to us in Europe, but rather consider all states equally within the framework of the Central Asia Strategy adopted in 2007.
This is our obligation.
The states of Central Asia, which only recently freed themselves from erstwhile tsarist and later Soviet rule, carry a heavy legacy.
Stalin's arbitrarily drawn borders, without regard for the ancestral regions of the peoples living there, evidently complicate coexistence both within and beyond the borders of these states.
Poverty, corruption, disregard for human and minority rights, authoritarian regimes, economic conditions that have pushed hundreds of thousands into
If we impose labor immigration and, as currently in Kyrgyzstan, admit conflict resolution through the use of force, we leave the future path of these states marked by uncertainty.
Here, we must not merely stand by and watch as the European Union faces challenges.
If we do not wish to lead these states down fundamentalist paths that would permanently expose their people to dictatorial regimes, then our full commitment is required.
In addition to the urgently needed material aid, we require contact with these states, offering active support to the democratic forces emerging there.
Let us give the Prime Minister, Rosa Otunbaeva, a chance to bring peace to Kyrgyzstan and guide the country toward stable political conditions; let us debate this here and vote on it tomorrow.

If the resolutions presented here are followed by concrete actions, then the people of Kyrgyzstan and the other Central Asian states will feel that we do not abandon them on their path toward the future, nor leave them at the mercy of undemocratic forces.
The expectations placed on us Europeans in these countries are high; we should not disappoint them.
The report is justified for four reasons.
The architecture of the service has numerous weaknesses, the intricacies of which will continue to concern us greatly.
The second point is that the European Ambassador will manage Commission funds in the future.
This exposes the funds to a wide range of risks, and the safeguards against these risks remain completely unclear thus far.
The third point is the transfer of rights of this Parliament to a Council regulation.
We are in this procedure effectively co-deciding, and yet we have allowed the Council regulation to override parliamentary rights.
Therefore, before presenting my dossier on the budgetary rules, I would like to state that I do not feel bound by today's decisions.
The fourth point concerns the question of how we actually interact with one another in Parliament.
We adopted a parliamentary reform in the previous period and procedures that we have now all discarded, even with the approval of the presidium and also with the approval of the president.
Why are we implementing a parliamentary reform that prescribes procedures, when in practice we already disregard and fail to adhere to them when it matters?
For these reasons, I cannot approve this report.
Mr. President! Mr. Commissioner, I am very in favor of green biotechnology, but cloning is something different.
That cannot be compared with it at all.
Cloning constitutes animal cruelty. Many colleagues have already stated this here.
I also raised this during the discussion we had before the summer break—only a tiny fraction of animal clones are actually viable.
And those that survive, of whom very, very many die very early and indeed in great suffering.
They suffer from malformations of limbs and organs.
They have immune deficiencies and are susceptible to diseases.
This means pain and suffering, and precisely this agonizing death.
How could we possibly be held responsible for that?
We advocate for animal welfare in this way, and then we want to support such a technology.
No, we do not want this, and that is precisely why we decided in July that we do not want cloned meat or any animal products derived from cloned animals.
And that is also only consistent.

Yes, we know that products derived from cloned animals or their offspring are likely not harmful to health, and personally, I assume they would actually be suitable for human consumption. There is indeed no shortage of meat produced through conventional methods.
Why should we engage in this cloning?
This is utterly unnecessary—this animal cruelty—and the population doesn't want it either.
The public does not want these products, and we do not need them either. There is no justification for cloned meat in the European Union.
And therefore, we wish to establish a clear regulation explicitly for cloned animals and indeed also for their offspring.
I want a ban on cloning and cloning products within the European Union, and I also believe we can enforce this.
Of course, this must also apply to imports.
I want to be certain that I'm not secretly being served cloned meat, as it is not labeled, as has already happened here.
Scotland. I don't believe it's possible to trace every piece of meat back.
With beef, it may be possible.
However, with pork, due to the large volume produced, it will not be possible to trace whether a clone was ever present in the lineage.
Fundamentally, I think yes, it is a principle issue, it is an ethical question.
And other colleagues have stated that we must also address ethical issues, and we are responsible for ethical questions as well.
Of course, there is the issue of animal welfare, but there is also another question.

Experience shows that I am a livestock producer, I have studied this field, and experience demonstrates that all reproductive technologies, once they actually worked in animal production, were ultimately applied to humans.
And so today we must ask ourselves: Will and can we desire the cloning technology in as near a future as possible, and can we also accept this in humans?
So I don't want that, and I can't do it either.
And even if, in the future, a replacement part, a clone of me, might possibly donate a life-sustaining organ, I would not want that.
I do not believe this constitutes an exaggerated horror scenario.
I believe that this would be possible in not too distant a future if we were to allow cloning.
Therefore, we urgently need a ban not only on cloning but also on cloned animals and their offspring throughout the entire EU.
I am not afraid of the WTO.
We can implement this.
We do not need to imitate everything the USA sets as an example for us, despite our transatlantic friendship.

Mr. President! Property, whether intellectual or physical, is a fundamental pillar of the market economy.
Therefore, I believe it is important that we consider how we can jointly regulate this issue through constructive consensus.
We definitely need a
It must become easier for consumers, self-employed individuals, and small and medium-sized enterprises to obtain the appropriate licenses here.

We need a single market for intellectual property.
We particularly need the original language to be available throughout Europe.
Every language is an important cultural asset, and it should be possible to issue licenses valid across the entire of Europe for languages, thereby enabling all citizens to use them.

Madam President, dear colleagues! I have taken note that the Council does not wish to discuss the multiannual financial framework.

I would like to inform the Council here and now that the European Parliament wishes to discuss this matter, and that the Council should not behave like a kindergarten and claim it will not respond to our questions or our proposals.
Secondly, I must ask the Council in this context whether it actually wants Lisbon or whether it does not want Lisbon.
Lisbon is not merely a technical adjustment, but entails greater responsibilities, involves greater task fulfillment, and consequently also raises associated financial issues.
In addition, the council has adopted resolutions that result in new financial expenditures.
This has been carried out by many colleagues.

If the Council wishes this and if the Council wishes that no more money should be spent, then the Council must also specify exactly where it wants to achieve savings, how it intends to achieve them, and precisely where it wants to redirect funds—rather than merely making general statements.

We must propose percentage-based reallocations and then leave it to Parliament and the Commission to decide where to impose the necessary adjustments, especially since our possibilities for reallocation or changes to the budget are extremely limited.

The tasks you are assigning us in addition, however, are so demanding that they involve no minor or incidental amounts, but rather, I will mention only the areas of financial stability of our states and their corresponding financial difficulties.
But if the program must be utilized, then these are not small amounts that could be reallocated casually.

The same applies to ITER, the same applies to issues of internal security, the same applies to major research questions, and the same applies to topics when I address priority areas that the Parliament has identified, namely in the fields of youth education and research.
In some cases, we are dealing with inadequate implementation; in other cases, we are also dealing with insufficient transparency in the disposal of funds.
All these questions must be addressed and dealt with concretely together, and not answered through silence on the part of the Council.
Mr. President, Commissioner Hahn, colleagues! I have an issue with certain parts of the debate.

When discussing mountains, islands, and sparsely populated regions, I sometimes get the impression from individual contributions that these areas are ex territorio and directly subject to the European Union.
But that is not the case.
Each of these areas belongs to a member state, which are sovereign.
And the implementation of European decisions and objectives remains, in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity, the responsibility of the member states.

And if I look at the European level, we have consistently paid particular attention to mountainous regions, sparsely populated areas, and islands in Commission communications, right up to the Lisbon Treaty.
And here in Parliament, many decisions were also taken to support this region.
Now that I hear so many complaints about ongoing deficiencies in these regions, I am left wondering how the member states have responded to the impulses we have sent out from the European level.
And what have the regions themselves done with regard to this, and have they utilized the European funding opportunities?

A new strategy is being demanded here. So I already feel sorry for the officials at DG Regio, who are expected to incorporate the wide range of problems faced by islands, mountainous regions, and sparsely populated areas into a single strategy.

At the same time, we are experiencing an inflation of strategies—Danube, Baltic, Black Sea, climate change, and so on—such that we will soon need a strategy for strategies in order to maintain any overview.

Mr. President! Distinguished colleagues, enough joking! I believe the real issue—something that has already been mentioned today—is this, and I am very grateful to Commissioner Hahn for having highlighted it: We must fight to ensure that cohesion policy and regional policy at the European level continue beyond 2013.
This is the focus of our work, and it is precisely this aspect that we should convince our colleagues in Parliament of.
And as a continuation of cohesion and regional policy beyond the year 2013, mountains, islands, and other regions will also find their place.
We should not, however, dwell on individual resolution proposals on this topic for which there is currently no justification.
Mr. President! Dear colleagues! First of all, many thanks to the colleagues who have worked intensively on this project, particularly to colleague Bilal Quadras.

Mr. President, my dear colleagues, esteemed Commissioners, I do not need to emphasize once more how important cohesion and regional policy are for living together and growing together in Europe.

Mr. President! First of all, I would like to thank Commissioner Hahn for initiating a new debate here as former Minister for Economic Affairs and Innovation, a debate which I believe can make a significant contribution to growth in Europe.
If every small and medium-sized enterprise in Europe created one additional job, we would no longer have unemployment in Europe.
We should therefore consider how we can enable small and medium-sized enterprises specifically to take on new employees and actively engage in international export markets accordingly.
But research infrastructure is also a major concern, just as infrastructure in the transportation sector is.
Here, the Commission faces major challenges, particularly in restructuring connections such as Paris-Budapest or north-south routes.
Additional jobs can be created here, which we currently urgently need in Europe.

Madam President, dear colleagues! There is no doubt that we have made progress in achieving gender equality in recent years. Nevertheless, there are still many women who are pushed into low-skilled employment.
This applies not only to activities traditionally performed within the family.
In recent years, deregulation has frequently transformed social insurance-covered employment into atypical, precarious, and insecure working conditions.
There, particularly women.
The European employment development from 2000 to 2010 includes 60 new precarious and atypical employment relationships, predominantly two-thirds of which are women.
Women are frequently denied democratic participation due to their part-time and precarious employment in companies.
Women today, however, are significantly more engaged and better educated than women in all previous generations.
Yet they still earn 25 less than men on average.
We have thoroughly addressed this issue in the Employment Committee, and developed sound proposals concerning women's employment, including their integration regardless of the nature of their work.

This means social security systems, the establishment of childcare facilities, aligning pensions and social systems with independent living, and organizing work according to the principles of decent work.
And all of this has essentially been incorporated into the report on precarious employment among women.
The Employment Committee is satisfied with the outcome of this work and requests approval of the presented report.

Colleagues, in the transatlantic dialogue there is much that unites us—these are fundamental values, fundamental rights based on freedom and democracy. Yet there is also much that divides us, namely the application and specification of these respective rights when transatlantic relations are at stake.

Therefore, I very much wish that this topic would be addressed and discussed clearly.
The European Union must not use the transatlantic dialogue to weaken its own promises and commitments.

Europe continues to voluntarily surrender itself to financial markets.
The EU member states must continue to auction their government bonds on the financial markets' altar and win the confidence of speculators through self-imposed austerity.

This time it was not the supposedly fraud-prone Greeks, but Ireland—the model pupil—forced into the European rescue fund to save its banking system, which had likewise long been regarded as exemplary, and as exemplary in its deregulation.

Mr. Trichet, do not listen to Frau Merkel's Department of Simple Financing.
I say: If you want to save the economic and monetary union, do not leave European government bonds hanging in the air.
Introduce Eurobonds before the next speculative victim falls.

Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues! Coal is part of the energy supply—this is undisputed.
It is also undisputed that competitiveness must be secured and that subsidization cannot be the right approach.
The issue now is whether the date of 2014 or 2018 is the correct one.
My colleague Rapka has presented numerous insightful arguments, which I need not repeat.
My request to the Commission is simply this: there is evidently a large majority in all the committees involved in Parliament in favor of affirming that competitiveness must be a criterion.

But that in addressing the issue of the term, one simply establishes the necessary flexibility, ensuring that member states are able to implement it, while keeping burdens on people, jobs, and economic structures manageable.

The only argument mentioned, which incidentally also contradicted the Commission's impact assessment, as the Commission appeared initially to support 2018, was the argument that climate policy considerations could play a role here.
I would like to emphasize this point once again: it makes no difference to the issue of climate change whether one burns foreign coal or domestic coal.

And my request to the Commissioner is to consider once whether this four-year period, in the Parliament and if such a new argumentation arises in the Council, might not lead the Commission to show some flexibility as well.
I have just understood the note to mean that consideration is currently underway within the Commission, and I thank you for that.

Mr. President, Madam Minister, Madam Commissioner! If we must draw a lesson from Copenhagen, where you, Madam Commissioner, are currently holding the presidency, it is that the European Union must speak with one voice at such conferences in the future.

There may be occasions when we differ here and there, but if we Europeans already speak with different voices in that region, our influence will lack credibility. And if we lack credibility, other people will not follow us.
Why should you, then?
That is why it was correct that the figures we agreed upon at the end of 2002 under Chancellor Merkel had to essentially remain in place and form the foundation of these negotiations.

I share your view that the ambitious targets of 2 degrees should not be seriously questioned today in favor of a significantly lower temperature increase, as this would make achieving these goals extremely difficult and cause people to lose faith in them.
I am greatly concerned that we are calling our intellectual property into question.
Thus, our industry invests money and manpower to develop things.
If we now go ahead and enclose this intellectual property with a fence while leaving the existing gaps intact, then the valuable components—namely our property—will slip away.
I am certainly in favor of technology transfer, but our companies must also see a purpose in investing in it, and therefore intellectual property must not be diluted.
Regarding obligations, I also tend to demand serious commitments from our partners worldwide.
But they must be credible, and they must be feasible for these countries.
Otherwise, we will do exactly the opposite of what we want, namely, they will distance themselves from us.
Appearing convincingly in Cancún is an important point.
The major decisions will be made later in Johannesburg, but I believe in Cancún we should prepare the ground.
I am a farmer by profession.
If the field is not in good condition, there will be no harvest.
Let us focus on the good arable land.
Mr. President! During this morning's debate on the European Council, there was an incident, which I myself do not wish to address further, as I was the target of an insult.
But I must say, there are levels on which I cannot be offended, and where I do not perceive anything as offensive, because to offend me, one would have to possess a certain degree of seriousness.
I would, however, like to sincerely thank you for the countless gestures of solidarity I have received since then here in this building.
I would like to sincerely thank you, dear colleagues from all factions.
I believe this is a sign of our shared commitment to community, democracy, and Europe, a conviction held by the overwhelming majority of this House.
My heartfelt thanks for that.

Mr. President! Dear Commissioner, dear Minister, dear Mr. Gauzès, and dear colleagues and shadow rapporteurs, I am truly pleased that we have now taken this step, marking the first time that a sector of the European financial system is being placed under direct European supervision.
This is also a step toward a strong Europe within a single market, toward robust oversight of a sector that contributed to the crisis.
I would like to make one remark on this, dear Mr. Barnier: currently, the selection processes for the new agencies are underway, and the new agencies will be no better than their personnel.

I can only hope that in selecting personnel, genuine expertise, independence from the financial industry they are supposed to oversee, and a clear commitment to the public interest will be the decisive factors, and that we will not see national quotas imposed again in the selection process, overriding these crucial criteria.
In addition, much still needs to be done in the area of rating agencies.

We still lack proper transparency regarding the information used by rating agencies, and we have far too extensive a reliance on regulatory ratings in the financial sector and in regulations governing the financial industry.
We face serious conflicts of interest; it is clear that there is market power held by three major providers, and we have no liability for negligently prepared
Therefore, I kindly ask you, Commissioner: It is good that the consultation is now underway, and we look forward to the results.
I also believe that civil society should participate in this consultation, but it must then be about making a courageous proposal.
I am already looking forward to your proposal.
Be as courageous as you have been so far, and let us jointly enact legislation next year that truly addresses, at a European level, the problems that continue to exist in the rating market.
That is what we owe to the citizens.
Mr. President, Mr. Minister, Mr. Barnier, colleagues, the past months since the beginning of this year have shown the damage that credit rating agencies can cause.
There is therefore an urgent need for action.
Therefore, I welcome the directive, and in this respect I also welcome it, particularly because supervision and registration of rating agencies will be entrusted to ESMA.
I believe this is an important and positive step forward.
However, numerous good and further-reaching proposals have been left behind during the negotiations on the report.
I would like to note this again here.
Therefore, the mandate of the ESMA remains the only genuine and positive innovation.
The proposals for a public database to facilitate the creation of ratings have been omitted, as has the mention of establishing a public rating agency.
The consistent and continuous review of established ratings by supervisory authorities has also fallen by the wayside.

The Commission had continued to propose supporting unrequested ratings in order to overcome the disastrous issuer mandates for the rating of financial market products. Nothing has come of this either.
I hope that these aspects will be taken into account in the forthcoming reports on this subject, which Jean Paul Gauzé and also you, Mr. Barnier, have mentioned.
Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues! First of all, my thanks to those who have worked on this report, particularly to my colleague Benson.
I would like to draw attention to two aspects and perhaps provoke a bit of reflection.
We agree that energy efficiency, energy-saving use, is the simplest and most reasonable solution.
The question is: How do we achieve this goal?
And I would like to ask: What has actually been achieved by repeatedly approaching people in Parliament with new and higher percentages?
Have we really achieved more and moved more?
Or why and at which points has energy been used more efficiently?
I learn that industries, which consume a great deal of energy, use energy more sparingly because it saves them money and because it is economically advantageous for them.
I am learning that people will choose this option when they know that this device consumes more or less energy, and when they can see the benefit for themselves.
Perhaps the method is not correctly applied by using percentages, but rather by properly focusing on incentives.
Second question: The Commissioner has just very impressively and clearly outlined how complicated the description of the term itself is. What is this actually?
How does this vary across the member states?
The colleague has just pointed out that Member States also have very different situations economically, geographically, completely different ones.
Is it the right approach there to work with a number, with a binding number, or should something more come to mind?

I believe we need to move somewhat away from the outdated debate in energy policy that relies on simplistic figures, binding mandates, and supposedly miraculous solutions, as it is evident that we have not achieved sufficient results.
Perhaps we should try the other approach—the differentiated, varied responses that take into account the different situational circumstances, yet still achieve impact.
It is about the effects, not about our state of well-being.
Mr. President! This provision, which is being discussed today, has received relatively little attention in all committees for a long time.
But now, at the end, once the result was clear, some people in this house have awakened.
Some unfortunately did not appear for the discussion at this time; it would have been very interesting to hear their opinions as well.
As a former member of the convent, the spirit of the convent remains very, very vivid in my memory.
has clearly demanded in many areas that the European Parliament must be granted certain rights in order to maintain and preserve its credibility both within the institutions and vis-à-vis citizens.
In this respect, what the convent had prescribed for us had still not yet been given.
The negotiations were extremely difficult, particularly those conducted by the Belgian Presidency with the Council, which made it possible to achieve some important progress.
I recall what colleague Lehne has already said—that was precisely the issue of the ongoing possibility of verifying whether the Commission has exceeded its competences.
Of course, there are some less than ideal arrangements, such as international trade agreements, but nevertheless I can agree with the outcome.
I thank the Belgian Presidency.
She has genuinely been very committed to ensuring that something happens, and our rapporteur has negotiated with both heart and mind.
13. I would like to make the following statement on behalf of our parliamentary group. This is a proposal submitted by my colleague Göran Färm on behalf of our group in the Budget Committee.
We did not re-submit this amendment request ourselves, but the Greens' parliamentary group re-submitted the request with 40 signatures from Green Members of Parliament.
We will abstain from voting on this motion.
However, I have reached an understanding with my female colleagues from the Greens. We are absolutely in agreement on this matter.
Since, however, we have generally stated that our faction will no longer be submitting any new amendments, we abstain.

I would, however, like to explicitly state that we support this point regarding the financial transaction tax and hope that our colleagues on the other side of the house will support us in the votes to come over the next few days.
Mr. President! I believe it is important that we do not use national tax systems against the internal market.
This is particularly important in cross-border services, where equal treatment is required.
We now have a negative example in Hungary, where retroactive price caps were introduced for crisis taxation.
This is unacceptable, particularly the financial, insurance, and trading sectors, as well as the energy sector, which are all significantly affected here.
I hope that this will not also be employed in the field of healthcare services in the future.
I would also like to ask President Orbán to review these regulations here, as they are entirely contrary to the concept of the internal market.
It is important that the EU addresses the issues surrounding migration and asylum law.
The Commission has submitted appropriate proposals.
Currently, there is still no precise analysis of the implementation of existing legal regulations.
Hardly any mature calculations and analyses exist even for the new proposals.
I may state that the EPP unreservedly affirms the right to asylum and the right to protection for those in need of protection.
However, examining reality unfortunately also leads us to the problem that the right to asylum is being invoked for entirely different reasons or even deliberately abused.
The right to asylum and subsidiary protection is not an instrument for general immigration into the 27 member states.
We must also ensure that organized human traffickers are not given the opportunity to profit financially—into billions—from the fate of people through our asylum system.
Asylum procedures must be conducted professionally and carefully.
Those who are persecuted must have the assurance of finding protection within the EU.
Therefore, the Commission has provided for corresponding obligations in the new accession treaties.
I consider many of these to be appropriate, such as interpretation services, proper medical treatment, and consideration of special vulnerability.
I would, however, also like to mention points that I consider problematic.
The authorities of the Member States are given far too few opportunities to prevent abuse.
The possibility of conducting accelerated procedures and border procedures should not be unduly restricted.

If an applicant seriously breaches their duty to cooperate, there are hardly any sanctioning options—quite the opposite. If he goes underground illegally, the Member State should no longer be able to terminate its procedure negatively. If he reappears, he should have access to an extended range of procedural options.
Even in cases of clearly unfounded claims, a expedited procedure should be possible pursuant to the second subsequent application.
This means significant cost increases.
Legal advice in the form of a lawyer is also currently, according to the wording of the Commission's draft, associated with a significant increase in costs for the states.

I simply and straightforwardly ask the Commission to reconsider the consequences of its proposals for practical reality, their financial implications, and the problems they pose for the administrations of the member states.
We want a high standard, but it must also be feasible and must above all not place completely impossible tasks before particularly burdened member states.
Dear Mr. President, thank you very much for allowing me to speak briefly once more.
I have a significant request to make of you, Commissioner Füle.
I would like to begin by stating that false assumptions lead to incorrect cost estimates.
We currently do not have declining numbers in the area of asylum seekers, but significantly increasing numbers.
This is related to visa facilitation, for example, with Serbia and Macedonia.
We are currently also witnessing a significant increase in the abuse of asylum rights in an attempt to enter a country of the European Union.

If one starts from false assumptions—that by contesting the initial proceeding in a higher-quality reform would save costs in subsequent instances—one automatically assumes that all Member States, so to speak, have a poor initial proceeding.
This, however, is not the case.
If standards are generally raised, this will consequently result in increased costs.
An increase in quality standards for all aspects, as well as the complexity of procedures, inevitably leads to higher costs in practical reality. I am happy to discuss this further.
Therefore, I once again kindly and sincerely ask you to take the concerns of the countries seriously, as practical reports from the countries are already available and being provided.
Mr. President, dear colleagues! This report credits the Commission with a successful competition policy.
It must not be forgotten, however, that unregulated competition can also contribute to rising unemployment and increasing inequality between the rich and the poor.
Unimpeded competition still takes precedence over the social needs of people in the European Union.
The creed of free markets cannot obscure the fact that they not only repeatedly fail, but are incapable of establishing social justice in a lasting manner.
A few procedures concerning distortions of competition and price setting do not solve this problem either.
A fair and simultaneously social internal market can only function with strong and equitable regulation.
More is urgently needed for this.
For example, it is unacceptable to subject an ever-increasing number of public service providers to private market competition.
The report provides guidance for the Commission's future competition policy.
Stronger consumer protection, the exclusion of public service provision from competition rules, and the control of cartels— all of these are urgently needed.
Moreover, a contractual clause ensuring secure social progress would be necessary, one that stands on at least equal footing with competition rules in Europe.
Mr. President! It is a truly significant moment that we are today commemorating for the first time in the European Parliament the Holocaust of Roma and Sinti.
Let me begin with the farewell letter written in 1943 by a 14-year-old boy, Robert Reinhardt, before his deportation from a German children's home to Auschwitz.
He wrote: "I have found my parents again."
We are on the way to the concentration camp.
I have mentally come so far that I can endure death.
I thank you once again for everything you have done for me.
Greetings to all, farewell until we meet in heaven!
Aier Upper

Ladies and gentlemen, this boy, like 500 other Roma and Sinti, never returned. And although Roma and Sinti, alongside Jews, were among the first victims of industrialized mass murder, today, 66 years later, the full truth about this remains unspoken.
The complicity of the majority society is often concealed.
We need the entire truth about this chapter of history.
The Holocaust against Roma and Sinti must be recognized and condemned as a crime against humanity, so that this history may not be repeated.
We must combat prejudices against these people with passion.
Racist attacks and acts of violence, such as those that occurred in Hungary the year before last, must be firmly opposed—and we must do so with civil courage.
Freedom and equality and solidarity are not the privilege of individuals, but the right of all, for human and civil rights are indivisible.
Mr. President! Distinguished ladies and gentlemen, colleagues! In my view, the crucial task now is to truly utilize the potential of law within the network.

We have the intention of fully exploiting the potential of a robust financial supervision in Europe, and we have doubts about whether, institutionally, these possibilities are truly being fully utilized.
We have identified shortcomings in the procedure. To date, as Parliament, we have not received the list of candidates in advance, but only the final outcome.
It would be simple to send it now to the executive directors.

We clearly observe a lack of gender equality in the appointment of positions, and due to certain incidents in the process, we have doubts about whether the member states are genuinely appointing this institution in the European interest.
This and the other points in our letter justify raising a fundamental question.
But beyond that, of course, it is naturally about the suitability of the individual persons, and each person must be assessed individually, according to their respective competencies.
There we see two major problems.

During the hearings, we observed that one of the candidates disputed the fact that it is an European institution, instead referring to a network of supervisory authorities, and even when pressed, was unwilling to correct this statement.
In response to the question of whether you are willing to make all lobbying contacts with the financial industry transparent, we have received no clear statements.
We have sent a letter to all three candidates, and for us, the voting outcome depends on whether we receive clear and comprehensive responses to it.
Mr. President! Mr. President! Our social democratic group wishes to submit the same motion.
However, I would like to recommend to my colleague Mauro and this esteemed House that we express it in a somewhat more precise manner.
We are dealing with an extremely large influx.
Today, Italy is as overwhelmed as any other Mediterranean country in dealing with the current situation alone, and Italy certainly will not be the only country facing the massive flow of refugees.
We can assume that Malta, Greece, and Spain will also be confronted with this issue in the foreseeable future.

Therefore, we kindly request that the title of the debate be formulated very precisely, namely as a statement by the Commission on immediate measures by the European Union to support Italy and other EU countries affected by the influx of refugees.
Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues! I warmly welcome the outcome of the conciliation procedure.

With the implementation of the regulation, it will be possible to establish, first, a high level of protection for all passengers in the coach transport sector, comparable to the standards of other transport modes, including passengers with limited mobility.

achieved, secondly, a high overall level of consumer protection is ensured, and thirdly, due consideration is given to the specific characteristics that this is a transport sector predominantly composed of small and medium-sized enterprises.

Thus, the extreme demands that would have rendered bus transport nearly impossible due to excessive requirements have been dropped, just as have demands that would have excluded the majority of member states entirely from the scope of the regulation.
Public bus transportation services, which benefit the general public, must also provide a high standard of quality for disabled individuals and people with limited mobility.
They should have a right to assistance, and for the sake of social integration, this should be free of charge.

Madam President! I would like to congratulate everyone, particularly my colleague Kancsjan, for his efforts in developing a compromise on this difficult file, which was both demanding and time-consuming.
There were successes, which have already been listed, but there were also disappointments, such as the scope of application and other technicalities, which have already been outlined.
With this compromise on bus transport, we have secured passenger rights for all modes of transport.
The next necessary step is to establish a common, coherent, and efficient framework programme for all passenger rights.
Fair and clearly defined rights are of particular importance for the EU's credibility among European citizens.
And in order to be credible, the coherence of rights for the various modes of transport must therefore be strictly respected.
It is unacceptable that fundamentally different criteria should apply to the rights of airplane passengers than, for example, to train passengers.

Therefore, when developing a framework programme, it is particularly important that we do not focus on the lowest and weakest standards, but rather, in the interests of citizens, keep ambitious goals firmly in sight.

Madam President, esteemed Mr. President, dear colleagues! We are in the midst of a major economic crisis, and we've been discussing this for months—yes, that's all true—but usually we only talk about it in headlines, and then we continue here cheerfully as if nothing had happened.
I believe it is because we ourselves in Parliament do not act concretely or honestly enough, but rather engage in headline-driven politics.
What actually does one do to promote growth and stability?
Are we ourselves also contributing to handling finances more responsibly and intelligently?
Do we really conduct a critical review of whether we are making the right expenditures, or does each of us not come up with a new idea every day about where else money could be spent?

If we lack the strength to channel financial resources into future-oriented projects—meaning conflict, debate, and decision-making—then it makes no sense to talk about innovation and research without providing the necessary means.
That is relatively dishonest.

And if we want to stimulate the economy, we must also pursue a policy that promotes economic growth, rather than spending every hour from morning till evening pondering how to disrupt industry or the economy.
We cheerfully continue adopting resolutions, constantly demanding new resolutions today that disturb the development of the economy.
Does anyone really believe that issues such as binding energy efficiency targets, to name just one example, contribute to driving economic growth?
Do you really believe that we have stimulated the economy with our decisions regarding energy that we have frequently made?
Or have we disturbed them?
I wish we could speak a little more honestly about this, and then make fewer decisions, but ones that truly move things forward.
This is more exhausting and laborious, and conflict will also become more likely, but that is the essence of democracy.
Mr. President! Colleagues! The demand for a financial transactions tax is not new; for several years now, opponents of globalization have been calling for such a tax.
Yet just five years ago, hardly anyone would have believed that it could be implemented in the foreseeable future.
But this week it is on the agenda of the European Parliament alongside a CO2 tax and Eurobonds, and I think that is a good thing.
The financial instruments proposed by Anni Podimata in her report can only be welcomed and supported by me, and I believe we will also vote on them accordingly.
They are urgently necessary instruments.
The consolidation of public budgets so far has focused solely on cutting expenditures.
Cutting public expenditures, however, primarily affects employees, retirees, and the poor—those who depend on the welfare state.
The revenue side of the public sector has so far been completely overlooked.
She is the second lever that can be adjusted to consolidate public budgets.
Above all, the main causes of the public debt crisis can be appropriately made to contribute to the repayment of public debt through the revenue side.
Consolidating public budgets unequivocally requires an increase in tax revenues.

In fact, the high level of public debt is primarily a consequence of the nationalization of private debts, namely the assumption of private banks' debts and the financing of the subsequent financial crisis by the public sector.
Therefore, the states are by no means solely responsible for the debt crisis.
It is therefore not only logical but also politically necessary to hold the financial sector accountable.
With the financial transaction tax, this sector would finally be made to contribute to reducing public debt, as it is recognized as the primary cause of public indebtedness.
This is an important political signal conveyed by this report, provided it is adopted in the form in which it has been presented.
But we likewise consider Eurobonds to be a sensible and therefore necessary instrument.
They contribute more to reducing debt than all sanctions or well-intentioned but more or less ineffective advice.

The complaints of some surplus countries that their interest burdens increase as a result of Eurobonds are, however, unacceptable, since these surplus countries simultaneously benefit from their exports to deficit countries.
This can then be criticized as a transfer union.
But anyone who wants a social Europe, anyone who wants the EU to remain united in the future, must at least agree in principle to a transfer union.
Finally, I would like to say to the Commission that I hope it will now finally submit the proposals for a financial transaction tax, which have been demanded for over a year. Thank you.
Mr. President, Madam President! The rescue shields were intended to protect EU member states against insolvency.
Yet they impose brutal austerity programs on the people and leave the perpetrators unpunished. Furthermore, the Commission is now also interfering in national wage policies.
Mr. President! It is absurd, and it brings tears to one's eyes, to hear here this formal argumentation claiming that none of this was ever intended to have social policy implications.
This position is entirely unacceptable. Wage policy is not the responsibility of the EU. This policy of the Commission is in glaring contradiction to the very idea of this European Union.
The European Union has never been a project for downward wage competition and social dumping.
It states here about wage rigidity, which is to be reduced.
I only want to speak plainly. Wages should reflect market conditions.
Ladies and gentlemen, they are speaking of color as if they were blind. People who formulate things like this have absolutely no understanding of collective bargaining systems.
They interfere with collective bargaining autonomy and reduce the much-vaunted autonomy of the social partners and social dialogue to absurdity.

I wish to state clearly that this Parliament is called upon to ensure the preservation of autonomous collective bargaining policy, to protect social dialogue, and to prevent further erosion of social protection.
We need measures against counterproductive austerity policies and punitive actions. Thank you.
You are a wise man, but you have not answered my question.

Therefore, I am asking once again: If Mr. Oettinger states that we currently have the suspicion that certain facilities currently connected to the grid will not survive our stress tests, I interpret this to mean that you are aware these facilities are so poorly secured that they will not meet the required criteria.
Why are you not intervening today already?
For me, this constitutes a real danger.
Mr. President! I would actually like to ask the two representatives from the Council and the Commission why they believe they need to respond to this issue with sound bites.
I believe it would help nobody if it were not stated quite explicitly that, precisely in preparation for the Istanbul Conference, the Member States of the European Union and the European Union
must finally fully meet their obligations regarding development aid, and it cannot in any way be tolerated that Member States are increasingly evading this commitment.
It is unacceptable that one billion people still go hungry every day, most of whom live in LDCs, the poorest countries in the world.

It is unacceptable that a large part of our development aid is not directed toward promoting and building agricultural structures, but rather that this share has rapidly declined in recent years, despite there being one billion hungry people.
And then talking about hoping that the LDCs will become more shock-resistant—I'm beginning to find that hard to believe.
I urge you to address the problems concretely and clearly, and to take specific commitments to genuinely assist the LDCs.
Mr. President! In connection with the internal market, public procurement law and the awarding of public contracts constitute a crucial component.

Mr. Barnier, if you say that in this context you also wish to promote social integration, then I must ask you: I have heard little about protection regarding social rights, little about the protection of collective wage agreements.
This belongs to the fundamental rules that, as you yourself stated, you do not wish to question.

If one wishes to uphold fundamental principles, future public procurement must ensure that basic social standards are protected, collective wage agreements are not undermined, and wage dumping is prevented.
This is part of a modernized public procurement law.
I expressly state that social rights must no longer be subordinated to economic freedoms and free competition.
Either we want a social Europe, or we want permanent conflicts with those people who turn away from this Europe in which they do not see their livelihood interests safeguarded.
Madam President, esteemed Commissioner Kroes! Radio frequencies represent a valuable and limited resource that must be managed with care, foresight, and consideration of society as a whole.
Therefore, the radio spectrum policy program holds a particularly significant importance.
Radio frequencies must be used efficiently, taking into account economic, social, and cultural aspects.
To achieve this, technical capabilities must be fully utilized across all bandwidth areas in order to generate the highest possible efficiency.

The foundation of radio frequency policy must be the Telecommunications Package adopted last year, as it also established that radio frequency management within the EU and its member states must equally serve cultural, social, and economic interests.
This must be particularly taken into account when harmonizing frequency usage within the EU.
In this context, I would like to point out that the implementation of the digital dividend so far has been carried out at highly varying speeds across the Member States.
A further opening of the UHF band, particularly below 800 MHz, should therefore only be considered after taking into account the experiences gained from clearing this band.
Many technical and financial issues have not yet been conclusively resolved.

And precisely the issue of interference and the question of cost compensation for cultural institutions and local authorities during technical retrofitting—such as in the field of wireless microphones—has not yet been satisfactorily resolved.
In allocating frequency bands, the various interests must be responsibly balanced against one another.

Widespread coverage with mobile broadband networks is, on the one hand, a fundamental requirement in the interest of European citizens, while on the other hand, ongoing development in the field of public and private broadcasting must continue to be regarded as an important instrument for information and cultural diversity.
We must set the right course for technological development, Europe's positioning in the field of digital information technology, and cultural diversity.
Mr. President, dear Mr. Rehn, I would first like to say that you have indeed described it: the situation has worsened.
The eurozone, the gap is widening between countries recovering and those that are faring worse.
And the costs of supporting the weaker countries continue to rise steadily.

In this situation, I have a few questions for you. As the Monetary Commissioner, I ask you: Over the past two days, liabilities and claims between central banks amounting to more than 400 billion euros have accumulated on two accounts of the ECB system.
This is because banks continue to be insufficiently financed by the capital market.
How do you assess these liabilities that are not provided for within the eurosystem?
Then the major problem why we cannot move forward with restructuring the debt is that the banking system, as Frau Ford also stated, is not properly structured.
The proposal for a European restructuring system has been delayed.
When will the Commission present it?
And as Economic and Monetary Commissioner, how do you assess this delay?
What do you do to make it go faster?

Then we saw, as we Greens had already predicted earlier, that the measures taken against Greece and Portugal will not be effective as long as the macroeconomic conditions in the eurozone remain inadequate.
We still face absolutely rising interest burdens on a macroeconomic level for these countries.
We are witnessing increasing capital exports and capital flight into some of these countries.

And we are experiencing rising resource and food prices, at least when one sets aside the recent fluctuations.

Besides the bailouts for these countries and the financial support provided, I wonder what measures are being taken to ensure that the weaker countries receive macroeconomic relief—on the tax front, on external demand, and on the issue of interest rates.

I would therefore encourage you to propose a robust package that improves the macroeconomic conditions of these countries within the eurozone.

Mr. President, Mrs. Kinger Göncz is a member of my parliamentary group.

I find it absolutely unacceptable that a democratically elected member of my parliamentary group is being compared to a bloodthirsty dictator.

I believe that Parliament must also reject this, particularly from a representative of a party that itself is characterized by inflicting hatred and persecution upon the Roma minority in Hungary, an extent of which is unmatched across Europe.
If a person here does not have the right to criticize Frau Kinga Göncz, then it is Frau
Mr. President! Dear colleagues, dear visitors! The cultural diversity of Europe is a valuable asset that must be preserved and further developed in the years to come.
To make this possible, we must confront the challenges of the digital age not only within the information and knowledge society, but above all also within the cultural and creative sectors.
Cultural diversity is not only an idealistic value but also a significant economic factor.
In the EU, around five million people are employed in the cultural sector.
This corresponds to approximately 2.5 percent of total employment.
The cultural sector is a growth area that is developing more rapidly than many other sectors of the economy.

The development of information and communication technologies is driven equally by the content provided by the cultural sector, just as the nature and quality of content have been transformed and further developed by technology.
We therefore need framework conditions for the culture and creative industries that enable them to develop sustainably, taking into account the challenges of the digital age.
We need a modern, accessible, and legally secure system in which everyone can equally develop.
Culture and the creative industries are a driving force for Europe.
Therefore, we must also pay particular attention to this area in the field of school and vocational education.
We must impart cultural and creative competencies to young people, as well as within the offerings of lifelong learning.
We must also strengthen the entrepreneurial skills of cultural creators more effectively, not only entrepreneurial competencies but also a solid media competence in the field of new technologies.

Mr. President! Dear colleague Kellam, dear colleagues from the Baltic States, dear colleagues! Today, we particularly remember the expulsions and mass deportations from the Baltic States.
These expulsions belong to the darkest events in European history.
And indeed, the past century was not only, and not least for this reason, rightly referred to as a century of expulsions.
And today we all bow down before the victims of these inhumane actions.
In this context, we demand a comprehensive clarification of Soviet history and, in particular, of the crimes committed under Stalinism.

Today's situation in Russia should not be understood as a political attack, but rather as support for coming to terms with one's own history, which, like the history of other European countries, is marked by both bright and dark aspects.
But just as we think of the victims from the Baltic states, we also think of the Armenians who were expelled from Turkey at the beginning of the following century.
We remember the so-called population exchange between Turkey and Greece.
A particular peak in deportations across Europe was reached in the middle of the century under the Nazi regime and the communist regime of the Soviet Union.
The expulsions after the Second World War also inflicted deep wounds, as did the expulsions during the Yugoslav Wars.
We must also not forget that many colonial regimes were characterized by expulsions.
We must demand a serious examination of the various forms of displacement and the spurious justifications behind them.
This also includes clarifying responsibility and punishing the guilty, to the extent that this is still possible, as is currently the case, for example, in the Mladic case.
But the decisive answer is European unity, precisely through and with the independent Baltic states, and a clear commitment to continuing the still-unfinished process of European integration.
And this historical task is what we owe not only to ourselves, but precisely to the victims of the numerous expulsions in Europe.
Many female politicians from all European countries, from both East and West, have worked on it.
For example, consider the Gdańsk Declaration, signed by Polish President Bosniewski and German Federal President Raus.
And because we wish to learn from the past, we must remain vigilant even toward initial signs of expulsion, such as in the case of the Roma.

We must reject all statements, such as the one claiming that Arab migrants should be sent back in boats across the Mediterranean, and we must resist those who seek to undo Europe’s achievements beyond borders and revive a new, narrow-minded nationalism.
Let us bow in Erfurt before all the victims of expulsion and deportation, but let us unanimously affirm our commitment to continuing European integration as the only real guarantee against

This is a policy that brought so much suffering upon people in Europe—and beyond—during the past century. Never forget, never again: these were demands that emerged immediately after the Second World War.
They have not lost any of their validity; they are best preserved within a shared Europe.

Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, esteemed members of this House! I had the opportunity to serve as a parliamentary observer during the referendum on South Sudan’s independence, and I was deeply impressed by the strong desire of the South Sudanese people to determine the future of their country for themselves from now on.
I also observed the great sense of expectation among people who viewed their country's independence as liberation from slavery.

Thousands, perhaps tens of thousands, of so-called internally displaced persons, who had been driven by decades of conflict in the south to refugee camps in the north, began the journey back to their former homeland in southern Sudan.

And of course, the people of South Sudan, beyond their desire for the country's independence, primarily wish to live in peace and to move forward toward a happier future after decades of violent conflict, during which millions of South Sudanese lost their lives.
On July 9, the new state of South Sudan is to become officially independent, thereby expanding the family of nations in our world.
The starting conditions are as poor as imaginable, and this has already been discussed at length here.
70 percent of the people live as semi-nomadic herders, 80 percent are illiterate. The conflict over Abyei is escalating into a military conflict.
There were already dead and wounded people there.

To make matters worse, rivalries between tribes and armed groups in South Sudan are also escalating, and according to estimates by observers, more than 1 million people have already lost their lives since January.
The wounded and those who have been violated are scarcely counted.
The Sudan was already among the failed states.
There is a risk that the state of South Sudan is already beginning to resemble a failed state.
I am firmly convinced that Europe has a significant responsibility toward this region.

Given the colonial past of those countries, joint European foreign policy is called for here, together with the UN and the African Union, and it can also demonstrate its effectiveness in this context.
It is not only about money, but also about supporting the delicate, emerging civil society.

And we must negotiate firmly with those in whose hands the fate of the country lies, namely the tribal leaders commanding military units, so that they do not disappoint the hopes of their people in the country.

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen! I would like to express my sincere gratitude to my colleague Bartolozzi for initiating this inquiry, thereby bringing Mongolia onto the agenda for debate in the European Parliament.
While the former Soviet republics of Central Asia are currently addressed in Parliament from various perspectives, Mongolia and its situation have received only very limited attention.

It was quite unjust, however, that Mongolia—the state of the former communist bloc—was the one that early on took advantage of its independence and embarked on a path toward democratizing its society.

In 1992, the Mongols adopted a democratic constitution, and since then several elections have taken place, which, according to observers, have generally proceeded in a democratic and fair manner—something far from self-evident for states in this region.

For several years, the Mongolian government has been attempting to combat corruption through legislation, and in 2010 it issued a moratorium on the death penalty—something even some major democratic states have not achieved to this day.
Nevertheless, the three million inhabitants of this geographically vast country, dominated by extreme climatic conditions, continue to suffer the consequences of an ongoing transformation process.
Thus, a rural population that is still predominantly nomadic is moving into the few cities that are not infrastructure-ready for such influx.
The country's mineral resources are not being extracted in an environmentally friendly manner.
Energy problems hinder civilian and economic life.
Pressured between the large neighbors Russia and China, Mongolia risks becoming even more dependent on these states than before.

The efforts and achievements of Mongolia in democratizing society should be acknowledged—here I see the glass half full rather than half empty—since they serve as exemplary for this region and demonstrate what is possible despite a difficult past.
Mongolia should continue to receive our support, not only through assistance programs but also by the European Union establishing a direct presence in the Mongolian capital.

I interpret this representation as a sign that the fate of Mongolia is not indifferent to us Europeans, and that European interests will no longer be represented from Beijing. One more sentence, please.

Madam President, esteemed Commissioner, dear colleagues, after nearly three years of intensive work, after far more than 300 amendments, after countless conversations and discussions,

After the most difficult technical negotiations and political trilogues, the political package—the major compromise between the European Parliament and the Council of Ministers on the future labelling of food—is now before the vote.
This regulation is intended to provide consumers with more and better information about food products, enabling them to make informed purchasing decisions.
But that is not all—Europe's food industry should also benefit.
Legal certainty, deregulation, and improved legislation are the objectives.
This is particularly important for small and medium-sized enterprises, and it should be noted that the food sector in the European Union consists of more than 80 percent SMEs.
At the same time, this labeling must be applicable to every food and every non-alcoholic beverage, ranging from basic staple foods to highly processed ready-to-eat meals.
That is precisely why a mandatory labeling in traffic-light colors is not feasible, as this type of labeling simply does not fit on basic foodstuffs.

Despite all difficulties, despite all political and ideological differences within Parliament, and despite all national egoisms of the member states, the compromise now achieved can nevertheless stand up to scrutiny.
Allergens will be recognizable at first glance.
The plant origin of plant oils is indicated.
There is a country-of-origin labelling requirement for pork, poultry, lamb, and goat meat.

For further products, an impact assessment will determine whether and how origin information can be provided, for example for milk and dairy products, i.e., products consisting of a main ingredient, meat in processed
Products such as these must be clearly marked on the front, and the same applies to formed meat and formed fish made by assembling or gluing together small pieces.
A package may not give the impression that it contains something that is not actually inside.
If a picture of fruit appears on a yogurt container, the yogurt must actually contain fruit, not just food coloring and flavoring agents.
In addition, the mandatory declaration of the main nutrients must always be provided per 100 grams or 100 milliliters for reasons of comparability.
Added water in meat must be indicated, the date of first freezing for meat and fish, and many other details.
Above all, all information must be legible.
We have defined readability, established a minimum font size, and instructed the Commission to set binding rules for further factors, such as the contrast between the text and the background.
At the same time, we protect craftsmanship from excessively high costs and bureaucracy.
Unpackaged fresh products are required to provide allergen information, but not the complete nutritional information, which would require expensive analyses.
The Commission originally wanted this, but that would have destroyed our national and regional particularities and specialties, leading to a European uniform porridge, which is why we prevented it.
But now we have all agreed, and the reputable consumer protection organizations are quite satisfied with what has been achieved.

I therefore thank all those involved, the Commission's shadow rapporteurs, particularly Commissioner Dalli, for his explanations, which I hope he will shortly deliver and which will untie the knot that has formed in our political discourse.

And I would like to express my special thanks to the Hungarian Presidency, which has achieved something that was almost no longer to be expected—almost the impossible, the consensus on this regulation.

Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues! Given the current extraordinary situation resulting from the financial, economic, and budgetary crisis, decisions at the European level should focus on urgent and essential issues.
Only in this way can we restore the trust of our citizens in the seriousness of our actions.
In my report, I discuss the strategy for preventing infinite human suffering resulting from approximately 35 deaths annually and over
2.5 million often severe injuries resulting from road traffic accidents, as well as the avoidance of an associated annual economic loss of 130 billion euros.
The number of fatalities alone corresponds to those resulting from the crash of 250 fully loaded medium-sized passenger aircraft.
What wouldn't we do to prevent that?
The costs of accident consequences considerably exceed the annual total budget of the European Union.
Therefore, I am confident that it is appropriate for us to engage sustainably in this area.

Thanks to the constructive cooperation of all shadow rapporteurs as well as many colleagues, we have for the first time succeeded in developing a unified, comprehensive, and coherent approach to improving road traffic safety through this report.
He takes into account the contents of the UN Charter on Road Safety 2010 as well as the objectives and aims of road safety efforts by the EU member states and broader sectors of civil society.
It includes, first, ambitious but realistic goals for the period up to 2020 and, beyond that, the vision of SEERUM.
Accordingly, the number of accident fatalities is to be reduced by 50 by 2020, the number of child fatalities by 60, and the number of seriously injured by 40.
This is in the fundamental interest of each individual in our society, as well as in our national economy.
We recommend that Member States break down targets and methods to the level of their territory and expect them to develop their own road safety action programmes.
Secondly, it specifies who is responsible for the individual activities and measures.
This is by far not only the European Union.

In line with the principle of subsidiarity, the holistic approach means that Member States, companies responsible at regional or local level, and indeed every individual road user, must assume responsibility in accordance with their respective duty to cooperate.
And thirdly, it includes how, that is, with which instruments, activities, and measures, the objectives are to be achieved.
Legislative proposals at the European level are only one strategy.
We cannot and will not do without guidelines, recommendations, and directives.
Particular attention has been paid to subsidiarity, meaning that responsibilities and competences have been clearly defined, even in cases where they are shared.
We appreciate the Commission's diverse activities.

Since they come from a wide variety of directorates-general, such as Transport, Economic Affairs, Consumer Protection, Environment, Communication, Research and Development, the Internal Market, and others, they are not always coherent, often uncoordinated, and sometimes even contradict each other.
Therefore, we propose the appointment of a coordinator for road safety within the European Commission.
This avoids the establishment of an agency for road traffic safety in a sustainable manner, but greatly increases the efficiency of the European Commission.
Both the selection of measures and their evaluation require a scientifically sound approach based on comparable, high-quality data, definitions, and statistics.

We therefore call, among other things, for a harmonized analysis of injury and accident causes, as well as the exchange of data throughout the EU, while maintaining a high level of personal data protection.
Six main action areas have been identified.
They concern the human as a road user, traffic regulations, vehicles, infrastructure, their intelligent interconnection, as well as the special consideration of vulnerable road users.
I request approval tomorrow.

I would like to see here a bit more flexibility and a bit more realism, and a somewhat better appreciation of what the member states have already achieved, without calling into question our fundamental demands.
Madam President, Commissioner! I would like to make a few remarks regarding the tax agreement between Germany and Switzerland, as others have already done.
Instead of effectively preventing tax evasion, which would be sensible, and closing loopholes for tax offenders, this has produced a Swiss cheese with large holes.
The proposed transition period of five months gives tax evaders ample time to transfer money from Switzerland to other tax havens.
Since foundations and trusts are not covered by the agreement, it is sufficient to convert the assets held in Switzerland into foundation assets.
Relevant forms are available at Swiss banks.
Some time ago, information was provided to German tax authorities regarding numerous tax evaders who are hiding their assets in Switzerland.
The agreement now grants amnesty to these individuals, and tax authorities may no longer use the information they possess.

Accordingly, a promotional video by Swiss banks states that under this agreement, customers will continue to benefit even more from the values of the Swiss financial center in the future.

This agreement clearly lacks not only the necessary alignment at the EU level, but also runs counter to the EU's efforts to increase transparency in tax matters, make tax collection in member states more efficient, and above all combat and prevent tax fraud.
Therefore, in our view, this cannot be acceptable.
We support this agreement and urge the EU to actively oppose it in case of doubt.

Do we want a union in which the key element is genuinely the fact that we say: It does not leave us indifferent whether people have to live in poverty in any country, any member state of the European Union?

Madam President, Commissioner, esteemed colleagues! It surprises me that we continually have to remind ourselves what is actually meant by structural and cohesion policy.

Cohesion and regional policy constitute one of the European Union's central policy areas, and by definition aim to strengthen economic and social cohesion within the Union, particularly promoting growth and employment in underdeveloped regions.
The necessity and success of cohesion and structural policy have been particularly evident in the years of the crisis.
In many regions, the situation appears even more difficult with regard to economic development, as these funds would not be available.
The implementation of structural fund funding takes place on the basis of operational programs, as already mentioned, which describe the funding strategy of the respective region.
The European Union, its member states, and regions collaborate here in a partnership, thereby creating genuine European added value.
The success of cohesion and structural policy also depends on the reliability of the programs and the available and committed funds.

Therefore, I am greatly surprised whenever, time and again, Member States—and, as we have just seen, also the Commission—attempt to treat structural fund resources as a savings book to finance all kinds of current policy initiatives.

This does not do justice to the spirit and purpose of cohesion policy and, furthermore, undermines the actors in the regions who are already working towards achieving the goals on the basis of long-term strategies and agreements.
Certainly, combating youth unemployment and promoting economic growth are important objectives.
This will be achieved only if the collaborative partnership between the regions, the Member States, and the Commission is maintained and if the actors in the regions are involved and remain involved.
For only they possess the knowledge of what needs to be done in their respective regions.
Cohesion and structural policy constitute the unifying, partnership-based, and solidarity-driven element in the cooperation among the various policy levels within the European Union.
It makes visible the solidarity of Europe at the local level within regions, and should not be relinquished carelessly due to political quick fixes.

Mr. President! Our group has supported the call for an amendment to the 2009 Regulation on biometric passports, requiring a review after three years and the submission of four studies.

And this is precisely not the case with nieces.
These data are also subject to change throughout life.
In children, they appear differently than in old age.
And precisely for this reason, reliable and serious studies and conclusions drawn from them are necessary.
Forgery continues to be carried out diligently.
We have now been able to determine,
As occurrences in Great Britain and other countries continually demonstrate, these chips are not secure against misuse.
They are cheerfully cracked and can cause lasting harm to those affected.
Standards within the EU are also extremely varied.
The error rate is extremely high, as in France and the Netherlands.
And yet again, the question must be asked: what good are biometric data to us if documents are forged and circulating?
If, therefore, one link in the chain is forged and the source documents are inconsistent, biometric data will not be able to assist us further.
A final example: the Chaos Computer Club in Germany required only a few minutes to demonstrate a forgery.

To accomplish this, the friends from the Chaos Computer Club required the cap of a plastic bottle, some superglue, a digital camera, and a little wood glue. In just a few minutes, they had cracked the chip. Such is the so-called security.
We expect the Commission to submit the studies, and naturally also the results and the conclusions that follow from them.

Numbers sometimes say more than long words, and to date, the European Patent Office—whom you have also mentioned—has granted nearly 2,000 patents on plants and nearly 1,200 patents on animals, developed both with and without the use of genetic engineering.
I may mention two outstanding examples: the authorization of patents on genetically modified and selectively bred barley, covering the entire chain from seed to plant.
Therefore, our farmers are affected just as much as our brewers, producers, and we consumers.
In my opinion, this example illustrates a devastating extent of patenting.
Secondly, for example, the so-called Long Life tomato should not be confused with its Long Life oil, which it has in its diesel engines.
This patent was obtained by circumventing valid EU regulations through a maneuver employed by the applying US corporation Arcadia.
What is patented is not the tomato itself, but its genetic material, specifically selected enzymes.

This reveals the tactics employed here, despite the fact, as you yourself stated, Mr. Commissioner, that the 1998 Biopatent Directive clearly and explicitly states in Article 4 that plant varieties and animal breeds, as well as essentially biological processes for the breeding of plants or animals, are not patentable.
We believe this definition must be clarified and interpreted more precisely.
Technical methods employed to support conventional addiction treatment may not, as a whole, render something patentable.
We specifically do not claim a patent on life.
And we need here a clear signal for greater ethics and greater honesty in dealing with exhaustion, once again opposing patentability of life throughout Europe.
Therefore, Mr. Commissioner, we demand the Commission's clear statement that patents on breeding material and genetic resources must be prohibited in Europe and remain prohibited.
Secondly, we demand a clear stance on Article 4 of the Biopatent Directive: no patents on life.

And thirdly, Mr. Commissioner, on behalf of many farmers and breeders across Europe, we demand a clear and unequivocal codification of the breeder's and farmer's privilege throughout the entire legislation.
