It is announced that everything will be improved in the future, including better compliance with tender requirements.
I recommend that we also review this in the future, and in particular verify whether all open positions will indeed be filled going forward.
This has been improved.
But if these positions are not filled soon, we would almost have to consider cutting resources, which would have a negative impact on Eurojust.
I nevertheless recommend relief. Thank you.

Madam President, esteemed Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen! What should concern us and fill us with panic is not merely the sheer number of young unemployed people in Europe, but the trend in unemployment.
Between 2008 and 2011, the number of unemployed youth increased by 26.5 percent, according to the International Labour Organization.
It is this development that urgently requires measures. For we are not only excluding an entire generation from the labor market, we are also cutting off an entire generation from a livable future.
If we give young people the feeling of absolute uselessness, we should not be surprised that they turn away from this EU, as they lack reasonable prospects for work and life.

Mr. Commissioner, I urgently request that you ensure decisive countermeasures are initiated. I already perceive much, but please ensure that the long-standing intellectual stillness does not now give way to operational frenzy.

When I read that the Commission is launching a program under which young people applying abroad are now also to receive financial support, then we are putting on a traveling circus that does not address the problem.
And conversely, it becomes counterproductive, as technicians, doctors, engineers, and healthcare workers are then lured away from countries that are wealthier and not facing this crisis.
Now, in this crisis, whose root cause is this development, offensive measures are needed—namely, the dismantling of this obsession with austerity that is undermining the economy in Europe.
We need a targeted growth policy; we need to strengthen domestic purchasing power, and public investments in education, research, and alternative energies.
That would be the way.
With the combination of European funding, it would then be possible to actually develop an integrated concept that provides young people with a perspective on life.
Final remark: This youth unemployment is also incompatible with the absurd idea of wanting to raise the retirement age to 70 for people.
We need a different policy that provides young people with access to the labor market.
Therefore, I urgently request this, thank you!

Madam colleague, you have just stated that everything in favor of the financial transaction tax would amount to speculation, and yet you yourself have subsequently presented very speculative examples.

I would like to ask you, in light of what you have said, what you think of these Commission investigations and the Commission study, which very clearly establishes that, until now, one has unintentionally paid far too little attention to the real economy and its dependence on

to financial markets that 95 percent of companies are by no means dependent on borrowing for funding, and that one can indeed turn the negative growth effect—previously discussed—into a positive one by appropriately channeling public investment into growth-enhancing measures.
Is this merely speculation to you, or would you say that such a well-founded, broadly conducted study by the Commission is also justified and constitutes a strong argument?

Mr. President! Yes, colleagues! The financial transaction tax is certainly not identical to the Tobin tax, which has been under discussion for many years, but I believe it incorporates essential elements of the Tobin tax.
At the very least, she embraces the idea of involving the financial markets in taxation, of taxing them, and above all of making them contribute to the costs of the crisis.
I believe this sends a very important signal to the financial markets.
This also sends a very important signal to our citizens, to our voters.

In a number of member states, there was a series of actions, petitions, and written initiatives addressed to politicians, calling for the introduction of a financial transaction tax at the European level.
I believe the Parliament has achieved a significant success with the majority emerging here for this report and for this tax.
Certainly, this is not sufficient to stabilize and regulate the financial markets as perhaps would be necessary, but it is indeed a significant step that has been taken.
I would like to explicitly appeal to the Council to take the voices of citizens, voters, seriously and to advocate for them.
I hope that a genuine introduction will take place, and that the introduction of this tax will not be blocked in the Council due to the British position or other positions.
I believe it is clear that the citizens of the European Union want this tax, and I think it represents a great responsibility.

It now lies in the Council's hands to act here, and I would like to use my speaking time to appeal to the Council to do everything possible to ensure that this tax is actually implemented and becomes a reality.

Mr. President! Dear colleagues! I believe that, despite all the detailed issues which we can of course discuss and which will certainly be addressed further in the legislative process, we should be clear about one thing.
That must be clear. Financial markets cannot function according to a model in which banks reap profits when everything goes well, but taxpayers are left to bear the consequences when things go wrong.
And therefore, it makes sense to subject financial transactions to taxation as well.
And it makes sense to do so across borders.

In Europe or at least in the eurozone, we will not experience upheavals; instead, we will consistently contribute to establishing a stable financial hub, one that generates what financial centers are actually intended for, namely, supporting businesses

Access to capital, enabling savers to set aside funds or contribute toward retirement savings, and thereby allowing financial markets once again to fulfill the role for which they were originally designed: delivering added value to the real economy.
All that drove us into the financial crisis were products that created no value for the real economy.
Therefore, it makes sense to impose a tax in order to contribute to the deterrence, sanctioning, and regulation of this behavior in such a way that capital once again fulfills its proper function.
That is why today, with a large majority, I am advocating for this proposal.
Mr. President! The reform of the generalised system of preferences was indeed necessary.
It is now good that longer durations are available and that exporters have greater security.
However, the criteria are too imprecise, as the Development Committee has already stated.
The Commission unfortunately refers only to World Bank enrollment.
The Parliament has repeatedly and critically addressed gross domestic product as the sole criterion in the past.
The definition of countries with upper-middle income levels may refer to countries with a gross domestic product of 4 or even up to 12 dollars.
This is therefore an enormous and far too broad spectrum, and it does not at all reflect the realities on the ground.
We certainly do not find it troubling that countries such as Saudi Arabia and Kuwait are excluded.
The reform in this regard was therefore good.
But these countries are high-income countries anyway.
Russia and China are countries with middle incomes above the threshold, and we certainly do not need to worry about their falling out of the APS either.
However, this category of countries also includes many poorer nations, such as Namibia and Ecuador.
Until now, the Commission had at least also taken into account the criterion of being able to form alliances, meaning countries that export little or only very few goods.
This at least prevents countries that depend less on the export of products and are extremely vulnerable from being overrated.
Why then did the Commission remove this criterion?
Countries such as the Dominican Republic, Fiji Islands, Gabon, and Botswana will thus be excluded, despite their economies falling under the criterion of vulnerability.
Why did the Commission not continue with the old criteria, which were already somewhat inadequate?
There is a growing suspicion that the Commission intends to force poorer countries into free trade agreements that are unfavorable to them.

The preference system, however, is an instrument for promoting development and cannot be replaced by purely trade-oriented instruments, in which the development path of a country or region would no longer play any role whatsoever.
Fortunately, however, the colleagues in the plenary session will still have the opportunity to support our amendment, which incorporates vulnerability as a criterion.
The new regulation also results in trade regions being torn apart.
Neighboring countries in customs unions will soon have to follow different rules when exporting to the EU.
This has nothing to do with our goal of regional integration.
The Commission should increasingly take into account development regions rather than individual countries.
We have also submitted a motion for amendment, which we hope will find broad support.
It is also problematic when countries that have benefited from the APS just yesterday are expected to fall out of the program overnight.
Transition periods are necessary, and we see in our own countries that mere months are insufficient for economic development.

Ultimately, we Members of Parliament must also consider whether we want to focus solely on speed in all draft legislation and then work with lopsided compromises, or whether we would prefer to involve the entire plenary session.
I believe the entire parliament is important for legislation, and also for democracy, and what matters is not speed, but rather the need for time and democracy to properly enact and adopt laws.

Mr. President, High Representative! I was also a member of the European Parliament's election observation mission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and I observed firsthand the great hope and expectation with which people stood in line at polling stations, eagerly awaiting their chance to cast their vote for a better future.
We now know that these expectations were not fulfilled.
Although the Congo calls itself democratic, these elections were neither fair nor democratic.
We must address this proactively, as colleague Delcheva has done in her report.
The former president, Joseph Kabila, has appointed himself as the voter. The opposition is being suppressed.
In the east of the country, violent clashes between government forces and so-called rebels have flared up again, and it remains unclear whether third-party states such as Rwanda are involved.
In any case, it is the civilian population that suffers, which for decades now has been unable to live in peace and tranquility.
The country's wealth is squandered in a swamp of corruption, and the Congo remains among the lowest-ranked nations in all international assessments.

The international community of states, particularly the European Union, must use its available means to increase pressure on the President of the Republic of the Congo to allow genuine democracy in his country. He will have the opportunity to do so during the next regional elections.

I look forward to the Congolese Army no longer persecuting the civilian population, but finally apprehending international fugitives accused of crimes against humanity, such as Bosko and Djangga, and bringing them before a court.
Internationally, all states should finally commit to no longer supplying further weapons to the Congo.
Without external pressure, human suffering and poverty in this country will not come to an end.
Mr. President! Colleagues, the TUPAC is intended to establish additional enforcement powers against crisis-stricken countries within the eurozone.
The European Semester, established as a framework for necessary and improved coordination of fiscal and economic policy, is to be strengthened.
According to the Commission's intention, member states should be compelled to accept financial assistance.
The Sixpack has already established extensive sanctioning possibilities that can be imposed on Eurozone countries facing financial difficulties.
The European Semester is already perceived by citizens more as a dismantling of democracy than as a meaningful economic policy coordination instrument.
At the same time, the German government is openly threatening Greek citizens with expulsion from the eurozone should they make the wrong choice in Sunday's election.
Why don't we stop making these humiliating and undemocratic gestures of intimidation?
In a situation where the euro crisis has once again become dramatically acute—I am thinking of Spain, Cyprus—it does not help us to put member states that are already in crisis on the spot.
We should finally develop solutions. The rapporteurs of Tupac have indeed proposed quite sensible approaches in this regard.
I have indeed received from Elisa Ferreira the suggestion regarding the repayment fund, which would be a worthwhile and sensible measure to combat debt.
However, the proposal by Jean Paul Gauzet of an orderly state bankruptcy is also sufficient.
Central, however, is that Europe undertakes a joint and solidarity-based effort to get the eurozone back on a growth path.
The strong countries in Europe must stop being complacent and pointing fingers at their partner nations.
You must begin doing your homework, investing in growth, and taking responsibility.
Angela Merkel's unilateral austerity policy has simply failed as a crisis solution.

Mr. President! Honorable Commissioner, dear colleagues! In June 2010, the heads of government of the EU finally recognized that the European Community requires a better-coordinated financial and economic policy.
At the summit, you could almost say you invented the European Semester.
It is entirely correct that members of an economic and monetary union must not solely concern themselves with how their policies affect businesses, employees, and consumers within their own country.
A European perspective is necessary.

A single market without coordination in economic, wage, and social policy presents member states with a significant temptation: to generate short-term growth and employment at the expense of their neighbors.
Such imbalances can economically, and also politically, drive the currency union to collapse.
And precisely such a situation is the EU currently facing.

For years, the German economy has drained its neighbors, providing no impulses through investment or wage growth; instead, Berlin has nearly driven the eurozone into ruin with a decade of aggressive export policies.
Therefore, it is encouraging that the Commission is now gradually addressing the core issue of the euro crisis in its country-specific recommendations and making corresponding suggestions.

It is entirely justified to call on the Federal Republic to invest more in education, research, and childcare, and it is entirely justified to demand that the Commission put an end to the abuse of mini-jobs and align wage growth with productivity growth.
This is a late, but important insight.
I hope, Mr. Commissioner, Mr. Rehn, that you will vigorously advocate this demand with the German government, even though there is no formal procedure for surplus countries.

Mr. President, Commissioner, esteemed female and male colleagues! Firstly, I would like to express my gratitude to the Commission, particularly Commissioner Barnier, for the simple reason that the experience from the previous legislative term, prior to 2009

It was clear that, with the exception of a very brief revival when the proposal from the European private company, the predecessor of Commissioner Barnier, Commissioner McCreevy, came forward, company law had essentially been in a deep sleep, and that over these past five years, in this important matter, which also bears significance for the internal market,
have occurred.
Fortunately, this has fundamentally changed.

There is now a wide range of consultation documents issued by the European Commission, an expert group has been established to address the issue of further developing company law, and we currently have a very timely consultation which, of course, we as Parliament must also address.
The Legal Committee has decided that we do not wish to present this in the form of a report, simply because a report would require additional time and, of course, be more detailed.

We have chosen the instrument of a written parliamentary question to the Commission in order to discuss this issue with the Commission and Commissioner Barnier, and simultaneously to clearly state our position from Parliament regarding what should happen now, in a subsequent resolution.
I believe it is necessary, for various reasons, to develop a new action plan for European private law, similar to the one we previously had around ten years ago.
There are gaps within European private law—genuine gaps—that fundamentally prevent the establishment of the internal market.
As an example, I would cite the absence of the 14th Directive on company relocation, which in my opinion remains a quintessential illustration of such a gap.
The Parliament has twice requested in its legislative initiative reports that specific proposals from the European Commission be put forward.
There is also the expert body appointed by the European Commission itself, which has likewise expressed the same opinion on this matter.
It is simply one of the fundamental freedoms in the internal market that a company must be able to move its registered seat within the internal market.
This option does not exist today, or only under extremely difficult conditions.

However, against this background, I must also simultaneously criticize the fact that the European Commission's current position on topic 14, the directive, clearly falls short of Parliament's expectations and, in our view, does not meet the standard of a qualified response to a legislative initiative.
I believe we can be reasonably confident that, as part of the ongoing work, if an action plan is indeed adopted, this proposal will hopefully be included in it.
Another important aspect of company law is the question of further developing company law with the aim of reducing burdens on businesses.
Here we already have very concrete proposals from the Commission on the table, for example regarding the topic of
Accounting, but I believe there is still more to be done and further aspects that will also be clarified within the scope of the consultation.
And here we likewise expect further proposals from the Commission.
I would also like to ask the Commission to revive the issue of the European private company, which is currently stalled in the Council.

If there are individual Member States in the EU—permitted, I may say so from my own perspective, to be frank—that by themselves, due to their own lack of resolve, are unable to overcome this deadlock here.

To move Europe forward, one must sometimes consider using the instrument of enhanced cooperation, just as has been done in other initiatives such as patents or family law, so that at least all other Europeans can benefit from this opportunity.
I would ask the Commission to reconsider this issue more thoroughly.
Another open issue is the question of applicable law, that is, a kind of Rome Regulation for cross-border issues in company law.
I also believe that this issue requires further investigation and discussion, and I would request the Commission to undertake this.
And thus also stands in close connection with
We have insolvency law, but it has already been announced that we will receive a concrete proposal from the European Commission in the foreseeable future.
You see, there is an enormous amount of work to be done in corporate law.

I am grateful that Commissioner Barnier has taken the initiative once again here, and that it is evident we will receive significant proposals during the second half of this legislative term, which can then contribute to progress in shaping the internal market.
I believe the Parliament supports this in principle and in approach.
Naturally, further discussion on the details will be necessary.
But therefore also thank you, Mr. Commissioner.
And now, of course, we would be delighted if the Commission would actually take up our initiatives and turn them into concrete proposals.
Mr. Commissioner, I greatly appreciate your proposal regarding productivity-oriented wage policy.
But when it comes to the minimum wage, you backed down compared to Frau Merkel, and I don't believe you regarding the issue of expanding employment in the healthcare sector.
How on earth is new work supposed to emerge there, when the Commission has already forced southern and eastern member states to reduce their hospital capacities by as much as half?

How can employment in the healthcare sector be created if, in the new country-specific recommendations for the European Semester, the Commission obliges certain member states to further reduce health expenditures?
I fear this point in this plan amounts to mere announcement politics, not a genuine initiative for new employment.
On the contrary, even today, shortages of medicines and medical supplies are already being felt not only in Greece due to austerity measures.
I believe that anyone who continually deploys new rescue shields for banks must now advocate for a rescue shield for people, particularly to secure medical care in crisis countries.
Mr. President! The mood among countless pro-European supporters like myself is one of frightened dismay at what is currently happening in Europe.
It is nevertheless encouraging that it is now much clearer what we are dealing with, and that eyes are no longer closed even regarding Italy.
You yourself know that you are caught in a number of traps—not only those of debt and growth, but also those of trust, and in the power trap, the esteemed Commission, and also the Council.
You have unfortunately so far failed to address the central issue: the separation of the financial sector from the real economy.
Everything we have decided here is repeatedly assessed in expert opinions as follows: The shadow banking system still faces no serious constraints.
Moreover, we are dealing with systemic risks. The issue of too big to fail remains inadequately addressed, and one hardly needs to say a word about the rating agencies.

I can only urge you, as you now work toward the Council summit, to also heed this advice: restore credibility in Europe that you are seriously addressing the issue of power between the real economy and the financial sector.

In a nutshell, they finally need to convincingly demonstrate to European citizens that they are working to bring banks under control and ensure that citizens are no longer left bearing the burden alone.

Mr. Colleague Färm, you come from a country that has rejected the euro, presumably on the central grounds that it wishes to preserve the flexibility of devaluation, precisely for reasons of social policy instruments.

Given the experiences you have now gained in Sweden, would you recommend that the Greeks also leave the eurozone in order to actively employ devaluations as a social policy instrument in exactly the same way?
Mr. President! I would like to sincerely thank the shadow rapporteurs and the rapporteur.
We adopted this report in the Environment Committee by a substantial majority—62 votes in favor, zero against, and no abstentions.
This shows that, in reality, we are actually very close together there, especially with regard to compromises.
High levels of protection, particularly strong consumer protection—especially for small, vulnerable children in our society—must naturally be the highest priority in this legislative process.
Regarding gluten intolerance, this is a problem, particularly in kindergartens.
When I take a look around, I have two children of this age, and given the allergies and intolerances present there, I believe we made the right choice.
And we should also discuss this together during the second reading, with the Council and the Commission.
I believe that we will then also achieve a good result.

Mr. President! A report on a traffic accident at the weekend involving a truck driver who fell asleep due to exhaustion and drove into the roadside ditch has once again confirmed to me how essential it is,

how a clear and consistent regulation of driving and rest periods, and their monitoring using a digital tachograph, is essential for us, for road safety, and also for the affected individuals themselves.
The digital tachograph represents a certain innovation; it brings new problems, as well as still existing old problems.
The new problems arise from new manipulation possibilities, which we must also gain technical control over.
The old problems are those we can already observe, so to speak, through the control or lack of control in various member states.
But what we are now regulating anew, in my view, requires significant consideration: first, that an interruption in recording does not automatically result in a rest period every time.
Because many drivers have to load and unload, and this, of course, also counts as working time.
The second issue is that the speed records were deleted from the file.
I consider that incorrect, as there are also significant accident hazards here.
We would also like to have weight sensors, and we would like to have a hotline so that fraud can be reported anonymously and tracked.
What I will also submit is a proposal that animal transport inspections be included in this regulation.
I consider this important because it is also a fundamental aspect of our legislation.

Madam President! Thousands of people could still be alive today or would not have sustained permanent disabilities if, following their accident, professional medical care had only been provided quickly enough—that is, within the so-called golden hour.
We would be much closer to the vision of Zero.
Well, the eCall system, which we are discussing today, cannot prevent accidents, nor does it relieve one of the obligation to provide first aid.
It serves solely to automatically notify an emergency dispatch center in the event of a severe accident.
This requirement must also establish linguistic contact with the vehicle involved in the accident, in order to prevent abuse and overload caused by minor incidents.
Only when the emergency call has been confirmed or no one responds anymore are paramedics dispatched to the accident site, equipped with a few but life-critical pieces of information.
The responsibility for the functioning of this rescue chain lies subsidiarily with the member states.
As long as the sensors installed in the vehicle do not detect an accident, the eCall system remains dormant, thus sending no signals or data and also not being located.
From a data protection perspective, it is also far superior to a mobile phone.
The system becomes active only with automatic awakening via sensors or with deliberate manual triggering.
It then transmits a predefined minimal set of information to a rescue coordination center.
Uniform standards and specifications across Europe will ensure that this data is recognized, assessed, and handled as an emergency call upon arrival.
Therefore, Member States must also upgrade and expand their rescue relay chains accordingly.

We want this eCall emergency call system to be based on the EU's unified emergency number 112, to be free of charge for users, to function throughout the EU, and to be compatible with emergency call systems of neighboring third countries.
It shall be based on an interoperable, technology-neutral, open system architecture.
These capabilities can later also be used to provide other intelligent public transportation services, such as real-time traffic information or parking space availability.
In recognition of the outstanding contributions of the automotive industry in providing private emergency call systems, these systems should retain their justification alongside the public 112 eCall.
Users can opt for such a system based on detailed information.
This may be accompanied by cost and cost allocation.
the use of data for workshops, insurance companies, towing services, or other service providers.
Mr. President! A small village in Franconia is known for its excellent beer. This rhyme highlights a minor problem we have created for ourselves today.

Because if this brewery or the beverage distributor is located 107 kilometers away from my place of residence, I no longer have any chance of him continuing to supply me with his products in the future without having to purchase a new tachograph or face additional bureaucracy.

And therefore, dear colleagues, I voted against this resolution today, because I believe we should not impose excessive burdens on our small and medium-sized enterprises. Fortunately, there were many points we managed to include that were positive.
It was indeed planned to accept only 50 kilometers within the vicinity of a regional provider.
That was completely absurd on the part of the European Commission.
So with 100 kilometers, yes, just barely manageable.
But what if it goes beyond that?
Thus, I consider it exaggerated.
And one final aspect: As a representative of a workers' organization within the CSU, I must also say that with this regulation, we are creating complete control over employees.
why
For example, the person who supplies goods to breweries for brewing or for beverages is monitored throughout the entire workday via GPS.
I therefore also consider, for data protection reasons, what we have decided today to be highly questionable, and I would like to emphasize once again why I voted against it.
Mr. President! Yes, many thanks for this question.
Why would I not wait for this decision?
I waited for the European People's Party's approval in the Legal Affairs Committee while we were still in the process, raising the necessary questions regarding legal certainty concerning ACTA.
And there was silence in the forest and refusal.
They did not want that. It is now obvious that the ECJ is being used for delay, that is clear.
Why did I mention the broad scope for interpretation?
Because you have actually asked me two questions, I must answer both, unfortunately.
Because one can literally read into and interpret anything from these broad formulations—internet surveillance, auxiliary police officers, ISPs, and so on.
And this means legal uncertainty. Legal uncertainty does not help our economy either, dear colleague.
Mr. Leichtwelle, I would like to ask you: Are you in favor of discussing the modernization of copyright law at the European level?
Or are you entirely opposed to the rejection of any form of intellectual property?
And how do you envision the E Commerce Directive being adapted to modern circumstances in the future?
From your statement, I understand that you reject any debate on these matters altogether and do not recognize intellectual property.
Is that correct, or how does it look?
Mr. President! It becomes clear that this is not about individual facts we need to discuss, but about the fundamental question: How do we stand with regard to democracy and to freedom rights?
And if we look at ACTA, we find that it symbolically stands for lack of transparency, absence of democracy, and violation of fundamental rights, because it was negotiated in secret.

and democratically speaking came about, because after that, the free use of the internet would simply no longer be possible, nor would the freedom to use generic drugs to the extent previously possible.
These are the reasons why millions of people took to the streets and have already voted on ACTA.
Yes, ACTA has been voted on in the streets, online, regarding the Petitions Committee, in numerous countries, not only within the EU.
Also outside the EU. And I believe we must not ignore this vote, which is a vote of direct democracy by many citizens who have elected us.
I can only tell you to file ACTA away, that would be decent.

Madam President, Madam President-in-Office of the Council! As rapporteur for both the Committee on Budgets and the Committee on Budgetary Control, I have concluded two procedures under your presidency: the budgetary rules, which establish a framework for the upcoming MFF period, and the OLAF procedure.
This regulation had been pending in the Council since November 2008, and you have brought this procedure to a close based on the outcomes of the Polish Presidency.
Therefore, I would like to thank you for two truly highly committed finance officials who have handled these files, as well as Ambassador Herr.
I will miss him; his efficiency as a negotiator will be missed by us.
You have truly earned your place in safeguarding the financial interests of the European Union.
Many thanks!
Mr. President, esteemed ladies and gentlemen, I am now speaking here as a representative of my parliamentary group, and I wish to support the motion put forward by my colleague Fjellner.
Ladies and gentlemen, we do not have many raw materials in Europe.
The raw material we have here is our brain and our creativity.
This requires careful handling of the issue of intellectual property rights.
The European Commission has referred this proposal, which it has submitted to us, to the European Court of Justice for an opinion in order to clarify outstanding issues.
I believe that respect for the Court of Justice of the European Union alone requires us to await this decision.
There is, on factual grounds, absolutely no reason to make this decision today.
No one will be prevented from voting against it once the court has presented its decision and its statement.
But the decision made then will take place in a different consciousness and a different awareness than exists today.
I do not mean that ghost drivers on the internet highway should dictate our politics, and that is why I ask you to support this motion.
Mr. President! As always, when colleague Turmes speaks, it is very insightful, and one would wish that what he says had far greater influence on European policy.
I wanted to follow up on that.
Of course, these project bonds are sensible, but only if they are oriented toward sustainability and if the necessary political foundations are also established.
We are indeed at risk of insuring risks through guarantees that we cannot politically control.

Specifically, an 8 km stretch of transmission network would be required for Germany's energy transition; only 100 km of these corresponding high-voltage networks have been built, partly due to resistance from citizens—interestingly, even from environmentally conscious citizens.
We must come up with something from politics.

One idea would be to create shared railway corridors, meaning not only combining high-speed rail lines with broadband networks, but also, perhaps above them, constructing corresponding utility networks alongside highways.
This, however, requires a far more comprehensive planning effort than currently envisaged, but I would like to at least raise this point here.

Mr. President! Dear colleagues! I consider Schengen to be one of the greatest achievements within the European Union. However, I am also fully aware that borders remain a very sensitive issue for our member states and will continue to be so.
I am aware that the problems existing between the member states regarding Schengen cannot simply be ignored.
There are various problems.
By ignoring them, one does not make the problems any better, nor does one solve them any better.
I live on the border with the Czech Republic, and here nobody would even think of wanting to close the border simply because we are experiencing problems.
But I also request support from the Commission in this area, not restrictions or reprimands.
The veil of uncertainty has been lifted, as we aim to ensure safety while maintaining freedom of travel.

And if we successfully conduct the border negotiations, I am currently also increasing controls at the borders with the Czech Republic, where one or another drug dealer is not only involved in a few cases, but in many

When we attempt to establish a transition in these cases, I believe it would be beneficial if the German Federal Minister of the Interior and his Czech counterpart were to meet in order to jointly address the problems existing at the border.
And the Commission should not intervene reactively, but rather positively and supportively.

We aim to put populists in their place, but to jointly address the real problems. That is why I also urge the Council to work together with Parliament to find a solution enabling us to successfully adopt the Schengen dossiers, so that we can ensure freedom of movement while simultaneously safeguarding the security of our citizens.
Mr. President! The cases of disproportionate border controls listed by Parliament also illustrate why the Council actually wants to exclude Parliament from Schengen matters.
The Council intends, in fact, to continue undermining freedom of movement without involving Parliament or any form of parliamentary oversight, without any form of public transparency.
No wonder, then, that you prefer to pat yourselves on the back in back rooms, away from uncomfortable questions.

No wonder the German government wants to completely exclude any evaluation of internal borders without prior assessment, thus deliberately avoiding any examination of just how severely freedom of movement is being restricted.
Schengen does not belong to governments, but to the people of Europe, and therefore also belongs in the European Parliament.

Mr. President, esteemed Mr. President of the Council of the European Union, esteemed Dimitris Christofias! Today, and for my political group, it is a special moment to welcome you here in your capacity as President of the Council of the European Union.
And we are also pleased that for the first time in the history of the European Union, a person whom we regard as part of our political family is leading the EU.
You are assuming the presidency at a time when the European Union faces immense challenges, and it is now crucial to set the course for the future of the European Union.
whether the Union will actually have a future, or whether it will prove itself—and this would be, in my view, a historic defeat—as a footnote in history.
Based on the discussions we have had with you, with representatives of your government, and with the chairpersons of the factions and parties in your country, I know that you are committed to this
that the new presidency has been exceptionally well prepared and that you are also fully aware of the significance of your responsibilities.
But—and you have made this clear here today—you deliberately wish to draw the attention of those in power to the living conditions of people.

I can tell you here that, after everything I have heard in recent years, this is the first time that a President of the European Union is not only doing exactly that, but also announcing and stating it openly. This tone
That is why I have long missed in official speeches the statement that this is about people, about those who are not responsible for our current crisis, yet bear the greatest burden.
It will be difficult to implement this approach precisely.

I believe, however, that we would already have achieved a great deal, and you will certainly do so, of that I am very confident, if you primarily encourage the people through your presidency and in the way you do so
exert, that they advocate for their rights and that they contribute to ensuring that the Council, that the European institutions are fully aware of this responsibility.
We continually discuss the euro and its stability, as well as the necessity—something I also recognize—that it plays a crucial role in the functioning of the European Union and the eurozone.
But this is an instrument.
For this, political conditions have existed, which have been defined.

It can never be the goal of the European Union to accept that people in the European Union must starve, that healthcare systems collapse, or that local administrations are forced to cease their operations, simply for the sake of the euro's functioning.
Something here is twisted.
It is not something we could designate as the goal of the European Union.
I fully support your assertion that it must be about social cohesion and solidarity among the various member states, as well as among the different population groups.
The European Union must not allow itself to be repeatedly used as an instrument or spokesperson for a small, specific segment of the population, nor may it portray itself in such a manner.

And one final point I would also like to mention, naturally, to emphasize once again that everything you have listed as your priorities is important to us, and that we will also support the points going in this direction, as I have just defined them.
Please do something so that people no longer have fear.
Fear is the worst advisor for democracies.
And I believe that some decisions made in recent weeks and months—particularly the elections in Greece and the referendums in Ireland—were also influenced by fear.
And if the leaders in the European Union contribute to people feeling afraid to make their own free decisions, then that is a poor sign for the future of the European Union.
Best of luck in your work!
I believe we bear a significant responsibility here, also as co-rapporteurs together.
The colleague will find it very important to work promptly and reliably on this file over the coming weeks and months.

At present, eyes are focused on us and on the Council to take this step—overcoming the banking crisis in Europe through another step toward European integration, namely, the establishment of a European banking union.
To this it must be said that the Parliament already demanded this two years ago by a wide majority.
We have conducted 26 trilogues.
We have taken a step in this direction, but unfortunately not as far as was actually necessary.
Additional billions of euros have arisen as a result.
It is good that the Council has now drawn the necessary conclusions and wishes to take this step further to deepen the eurozone.
It is praiseworthy that the Commission has developed the proposals so quickly.
However, establishing the Council's decision that the ECB should be the supervisor for the euro area entails certain difficulties.
In the Council, this leads to unanimity, and it results in the Parliament not having co-decision power on this file.
Based on the experience with the supervision package, this is not acceptable.

And therefore it is important that we work swiftly here, but also negotiate this proposal on changes to the ECB together with the necessary changes to the existing supervisory framework as a package, thus effectively establishing the principle of democratic involvement of the Parliament.
Democratically, there are further challenges in addition.
The ECB is independent in monetary policy, but not in fiscal policy, for which it currently has no responsibilities.

If it is to be held accountable in bank supervision, the Parliament must have full budgetary rights, full oversight rights, rights of questioning, and appointment rights, and I am confident that the Parliament will secure these rights with a broad majority.

Beyond these complex issues concerning the internal market, consumer protection, and the balance between European authority and financial responsibility, we will thoroughly discuss them in the coming weeks.
Mr. President! By proposing to exclude developing countries from preferential market access, the Commission is attempting to force these countries into free trade agreements.

They are not the free access arrangements implied by the term "economic partnerships," but rather focus solely on the interests of the EU and not on the development interests of the partner countries.
This runs completely counter to the EU's goal of reducing poverty and aligning all external policy instruments with this objective.
Countries such as Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire, and Namibia would be severely affected by this restriction.
Regional integration projects such as those in Southern Africa would collapse if Kenya were to enter into such an agreement.
The South-South Cooperation would be at an end.
We cannot advocate for free trade in the world while merely meaning that other countries should kindly purchase our goods.
If the Commission truly believes that trade can promote development, then it should not block it.
The Development Committee of this House has recommended rejecting the Commission's proposal.
We should support this in plenary session.
Mr. President! There are enormous human rights problems in Central America, as well as in Peru and Colombia.
And as Members of the European Parliament, we cannot simply close our eyes to it.
We will examine in detail the roadmap on human rights, which the EP has requested in the cases of Colombia and Peru, once it has been drawn up.
It must be substantive and binding, and include initial results such as the conviction of criminals and the safe return of displaced persons to their homeland.
And of course, it must also be available prior to the vote on the agreement.
We Greens consider it appropriate that the Committee on Trade demands environmental and social standards in the regulatory proposal on safeguard clauses, and not just tonnage restrictions on bananas.
The trilogue must also confirm these, otherwise the regulation is worthless.
The EU must overall do much more to keep the situation on the ground and, above all, its impact on the local population in view.
And that is why we Greens demand a proper impact assessment before every negotiation, one that examines not only the economic effects on the EU but also the implications for human rights.

We, as the Commission, as a European institution, stand for the prevention of people being driven further into poverty, for the avoidance of shortened working hours, and for the preservation of ecological and social standards.

They must be punished for it today and must endure sanctions.
It is unsuitable for strengthening a European community, a community of solidarity.
I do not ascribe that to you, Mr. Barroso, but this is an approach that runs through many countries and unfortunately, for example, also recurs repeatedly in my own approach

Madam President! The new directive establishes a framework for handling books, newspapers, artworks, films, musical compositions, and TV productions that are protected by copyright, but for which the rights holder cannot be identified or located.
Accordingly, books, films, photographs, or other intellectual creations for which no rights holder can no longer be identified are to be made available to the public as well.
And thus, a cultural treasure is uncovered.
For us in the Committee on Culture, it was particularly important in this legislative initiative to emphasize the special significance of the diversity of the cultural heritage of the European Union.

Countless unused works are stored in the archives of our museums, libraries, publishers, and broadcasting organizations, and until now they have not been made available simply because the rights holders are unknown.
This changes with the directive.
It will enable future use of this cultural asset, provided that a careful search is conducted prior to publication.
This is a very important point, so that it is not casually stated: We cannot locate the rights holder.
For a balance had to be struck with the actual rights of creators of works and the public's need to be able to use such works where the legal situation cannot be clarified.
A particular emphasis for us also lies in ensuring that both known and unknown rights holders are fully protected.
And where rights holders cannot be identified, the draft makes the use of digitized orphan works possible.
Projects such as Europeana and other major European digitization initiatives, as well as our museums, archives, educational institutions, and public broadcasting services, will be launched.
In the future, it must also be about opening up orphaned works in the archives of private publishers to the public.
In my view, this draft guideline and the compromise now reached truly represent a compromise and a first step in the right direction.
I believe that the cultural treasure, which is too high, holds immense potential.
And we should continue working on this when revising the directive in two years' time.
Mr. President, esteemed Commissioner, Mr. President-in-Office of the Council, I would be pleased if in the 2013 proposal we also had clarity
Signals from the Council would show how we could enhance growth and competitiveness potential in Europe, and above all, how we could provide employment again for young people.
Could you please inform us which budget lines you intend to allocate to promoting youth employment and placing entrepreneurship and small and medium-sized enterprises at the center?
We know that the CIP programme is the essential prerequisite for this. Here, I only see cuts proposed by the Council.
or the Erasmus for Young Entrepreneurs programme or the programme for media pluralism—here, there are only cuts.
Do you really believe that we can overcome the crisis in Europe by making cuts precisely in those areas that ensure growth and employment?
I have heard that the Council has reportedly proposed a growth and employment package.
Where in this household, as presented by the Council, can it be found?
Mr. President! Nordseewerke Emmen was forced to file for insolvency last Wednesday, as the guarantee provided by the Lower Saxony state government was unexpectedly not extended.
This company, with 700 qualified positions, is undergoing a transformation process, evolving from a shipyard into a supplier for offshore wind energy.

And now the idea is that this company can be rescued through a mass loan, and jobs can also be saved, as the 700 colleagues at Siak and Nordseewerke have already had to accept significant reductions in wages.

Therefore, I kindly ask the Commission to review this procedure sympathetically, yet promptly, so that these 700 jobs are not put at risk, as these 700 jobs are needed to organize the energy transition and to secure future-oriented energy production in Europe's seas.
Please ensure your colleagues there do not suffer as a result of the state government's poor crisis management.
The goods from Villueus often appear harmless, but in the hands of authoritarian regimes, they can be as dangerous as machine guns or tanks.
We undermine our foreign policy objectives when we send surveillance technologies to authoritarian regimes that can use them to control and suppress democratic movements.
It was European mobile phone software from Nokia Siemens Networks that fell into the hands of Iranian security forces, enabling them to suppress the green protest movement.
Protesters were massively abducted, arrested, tortured, and sentenced to death.
And this is just one example among many.
Moreover, the Commission also promotes these dubious dual-use goods through its action plan for innovative and competitive security sectors, as stated.
The objective is to achieve, as it is phrased, a better exploitation of the synergies between security and defense technologies.
We risk literally selling off human rights, as well as our own security interests, for the sake of a questionable industrial policy.
Therefore, I kindly ask you to support the amendments submitted by us, the Social Democrats, and ALDE.
I would like to ask my fellow female colleague, Frau Regner: What do you believe trade unions could do to combat youth unemployment, for example in Spain and Portugal?
Demonstrations will be insufficient.
What active policy could the internal market contribute in this regard?
Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues! I believe that with today's debate on MiFID we are entering the core area of financial market regulation.
in this legislative term, it is about regulating financial markets, rather than focusing solely on individual products, as we have done in the past.
I would only like to mention a few key points.
We didn't make things very easy for each other in our work last year.

We held a hearing in the committee, conducted a survey of all stakeholders and interested groups, and based on this, developed a result through various processes, working documents, and similar means, which we are presenting to the plenary session for voting today and tomorrow.
I would like to highlight a few key points.
Theme number one: Market structure.

On the one hand, we must naturally acknowledge that considerable developments have taken place in the financial markets in recent years, and that it is our interest as Parliament that every trading of financial instruments takes place within the MiFID rules, which is why we also support a new category under many conditions

as the so-called Organised Trading Facilities have approved, where only non-equities are to be traded, where proprietary trading is to be prohibited, and where, in accordance with the execution, transactions, and interaction with clients, appropriate execution may take place at their own discretion.
I believe that by doing so we will make an important contribution toward ensuring that the entire segment of OTC instruments traded is conducted within the MiFID framework.
Another major issue, which was not addressed in the previous MiFID, is so-called algorithmic trading or high-frequency trading.

Here too, our aim was to establish clear rules of the game, ensuring that this area— which can be extremely dangerous and is also susceptible to manipulative use— is subject to strict regulations, including minimum holding periods.

with trade interruption options, the ability to test algorithms, the possibility of supervisory oversight of algorithms, the introduction of so-called tick size regime, a minimum price increment, and corresponding fee structures.
Another topic I would like to address is the area of genuine futures markets.

I do believe we bear a special responsibility here in ensuring that the speculative component in genuine futures markets is restricted in such a way that we do not contribute, through flawed regulations, to the situation where people in the third and fourth worlds can no longer afford to buy food.
That is why we have adopted a two-part approach aimed at curbing speculation while avoiding harm to the real economy.
With position limits, with strict upper bounds on the number of contracts or positions, we aim to achieve this.
We also want to ensure, through a so-called Position Check System, that those who can demonstrate they genuinely need a product do not suddenly engage in speculative trading in this market.
Another topic that occupies us greatly is the area of investor protection.
I would like to point out here only that we have adopted a very broad approach when it comes to ensuring consumer and investor protection.
The focus here is on identifying the needs of specific customer groups.
Not every small saver needs access to all highly complex documents.

The issue involves defining appropriate products for target groups, conducting product tests, telephone recordings, corresponding consultations, disclosure obligations, but I also wish to briefly point out in the area of commissions the principle of subsidiarity.
Member States should have the opportunity, according to their respective structures, to make appropriate adjustments.
I think we are on the right track there.
After my term has ended, I would like to thank everyone who has contributed here.
I find it regrettable that one is given only four minutes of speaking time for such a complex dossier.
Therefore, I would like to thank all my colleagues who have contributed here, as well as the Commission for its very constructive role over the past weeks and months.
I hope that with a strong mandate from the plenary tomorrow, we will be able to enter negotiations with the Council and conclude this important file as soon as possible. Thank you.
Mr. President! Dear colleagues! Thank you first of all for the generally good cooperation on this report.
We have been able to further improve a Commission proposal made at key locations, which had already taken significant steps in the right direction.
In the area of market structures, there will be greater transparency, with more comprehensive transparency, particularly in the field of derivatives trading.

Under the MiFID framework, the decisions on high-frequency trading in our compromise go significantly further than the Commission's original proposal and deserve support, particularly from the Parliament as a whole.
And even on issues that have provoked significant public criticism, the report points in the right direction.

Even in the area of commodity and food speculation, after lengthy and difficult debates—something that must also be acknowledged—current regulations are now significantly stronger than the Commission's proposals, as position limits are now being established as binding.
We do, however, require further changes in this area in order to close remaining gaps.
I also hope for further input here during the plenary vote, and ultimately during the trilogue.
It is encouraging that Mr. Barnier has already signaled his support for the Parliament's more far-reaching proposal.
Where the report, however, has weaknesses is in the area of consumer protection.
We now have the possibility to ban products—that is progress—but we still have no changes in the area of commission-driven advice.
The distribution of financial products remains largely not oriented toward the interests of customers, but is instead strongly driven by which product generates the highest commissions.
In Germany in particular, but across the entire EU, consumers have lost a great deal of money.
Consider the open-end real estate funds.
For citizens in countries such as France, Italy, Spain, and Germany, relatively little will change even after our compromise.
The fundamental principle will continue to be commission orientation.
Referencing subsidiarity here is a bit simplistic.
Here, I would have preferred stricter rules.
Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues! Yes, what takes a long time ultimately turns out well—I can only say that.
The report on the type approval of vehicles in category L has ultimately turned out to be a good report after all.
The work took longer overall than any of us had anticipated, and in particular longer than we had hoped, and there were far more setbacks than ultimately suited us.
But at this point, I would like to once again sincerely thank the rapporteur, Mr. Van de Kamp, for the truly cooperative and constructive collaboration.
Nevertheless, together we have achieved much in the negotiations with the member states.
As rapporteur for my political group, I recommend approving this report. Nevertheless, it could have been much better.
And by better, my highly esteemed colleagues, I mean safer.
For us social democrats, vehicle safety and, consequently, the safety of drivers has been a top priority from the outset, particularly the safety of scooter and motorcycle riders, both women and men.
The number of motorcyclists involved in accidents has remained constant for years.
Generally, the number of accident fatalities in Europe is declining, except in the case of motorcycle accidents.
Reducing the number of motorcyclists and scooter riders killed is indeed a matter close to my heart.
Fewer deaths on Europe's roads—that was and still is my goal.
Every dead person is one too many.
This issue must be thoroughly addressed, particularly in a regulation so advantageous for the internal market.
I remain convinced that this can be achieved through effective technologies such as anti-lock braking systems, or ABS.
And I am equally convinced of the cost-effectiveness.
It is simply not true that ABS is too expensive.

Nevertheless, we have achieved that from 2016 onward, all new motorcycle types and from 2017 onward all existing motorcycle types with engines of 125 cc displacement must be equipped with ABS by the manufacturer as a mandatory requirement.
On motorized scooters, manufacturers may equip ABS or combined braking systems.
In this respect, we must unfortunately admit that we have not achieved any improvement over the Commission's proposal.
I had hoped for much more here, but the Commission and the Member States have, to put it mildly, shown themselves to be very sluggish.
Nevertheless, we have achieved that in 2019 the Commission presented a report.
The report must be based on traffic accident data from the Member States and should examine the mandatory fitting of ABS for motorcycles.
Furthermore, it is sent to the Parliament and the Council.
On the basis of this report, the Commission will then consider making the installation of ABS in trailers mandatory.
You see, the topic remains very, very alive.
Therefore, we will endeavor to close the binding gap in legislation starting in 2019.
I am certain that the collected data will support my claim.
I am convinced that this is indeed a good day for motorcycle riders across Europe and that accident rates will decrease in the future.
Mr. President! Mr. Matthias Groote, there is no technology without risks. There is no perfect energy generation technology.
And I believe this also applies to what we are discussing here today.
I am very in favor of our discussing this in a rational manner.
What we are currently experiencing is a new influx into energy markets through unconventional resources, primarily in gas, but also to some extent in oil.

I believe it is neither appropriate to lapse into great euphoria about the future of gas. Equally inappropriate is what some of my colleagues from the Greens and the Left have done here—painting dire and terrifying scenarios that have nothing at all to do with reality.

I believe we must keep in mind that these unconventional resources have the potential to transform all energy supply relationships we know—between suppliers and demanders—and that the economic calculations for previously dominant energy sources are now being called into question.
We must confront this development.

I believe we should recognize that this could also present an opportunity for Europe, and not merely a risk. We should prepare ourselves to positively embrace and utilize new technologies, to fairly weigh opportunities against risks, and to avoid falling into ideological debates.
Mr. Commissioner, your response does not quite satisfy me.
For I consider a period of one year between being notified that the complaint has been received and possibly receiving a response so late to be excessively long.
I believe a complainant who takes the trouble—someone who has usually already exhausted the entire legal remedy available in their member state—deserves more.
We rely on citizens to become actively involved in the European Union.
If they do it in this form, we should treat them accordingly.

And I believe it is necessary and indispensable in the long term that we have a procedure here that is clearly formalized and clearly defines rights. This is currently an explanation of how the Commission intends to handle this.
On another point, I have no objection to the approach of attempting, together with the Member State, to achieve a form of mutually agreeable clarification, provided it serves the purpose.
Only this will not always be sufficient.
We observe that, for example, in economic matters progress is relatively swift when one company sues another for violating EU law.

But when it comes, for example, to environmental issues and similar matters, where a very large number of citizens demand the involvement of the European Union, or indeed social rights, progress becomes somewhat more difficult.
I believe these facts also need to be clearly taken into account.
And I wish for your next report that we have, so to speak, a clear formalization of the complainant's rights vis-à-vis the Commission, which is also genuinely clear and effectively communicated.
Once again, if we truly want European law to be applied, after we have gone through such lengthy procedures here in the European Parliament, in the Council
have jointly decided. Then we must inform those who are responsible, who truly engage themselves in a European manner, as thoroughly and precisely as possible.
This is a very, very important concern to me.
And I will also carefully observe once again what has developed here the next time.
Mr. President, ladies and gentlemen! Five years ago, the EU's Central Asia Strategy was launched.
When asking today about the assessment of this strategy, the evaluation proves ambivalent.
We see how the member states individually strive for good economic relations, particularly with the resource-rich countries in this region, while at the same time these efforts make no progress.
to accompany Member States in their transformation process towards modern democratic societies, or as the recent events in Kazakhstan demonstrate, where development has even been marked by setbacks.

Nevertheless, or precisely because of this, the European Union and its member states must more strongly express their influence in this region together, especially and particularly towards Kazakhstan, which has now become a major supplier for numerous EU countries.

Isolating Kazakhstan from the EU would only enable competing powers seeking influence in this geopolitically significant country—where adherence to civil and human rights is less of a priority—to further strengthen their influence compared to previous levels.
The linkage of economic interests with advocacy for the protection of civil rights lies at the heart of the Central Asia strategy.
This should also serve as the basis for further negotiations on a Partnership and Cooperation Agreement with Kazakhstan.
The present report provides a good orientation on this matter.

Madam President! Ladies and gentlemen of the Council, who are not present here today—perhaps you are following us via the Parliament's video stream, since clearly you have neither the capacity nor the will to speak with us live.
I regard the absence of the Council here and today as a gross disregard of Parliament.

Rather than avoiding this debate, you would be better advised to reveal your secret reason for opposing any improvement in the protection of personal data in the fields of police and justice through a directive.

For years, police and justice authorities have been, as we know, an Eldorado for data protection violations—not only in the case of SWIFT, but for example in daily practice, where data on citizens, such as those demonstrating against Nazis, are unlawfully collected and stored.
We need identical protection standards for the public and private sectors, as well as for police and judiciary, in order to guarantee civil rights.
If you demand of the Council that it constructively contribute to data protection-relevant regulations such as EU PNR or Europol, then the same should presumably also apply to the Council in reverse.
That is why I demand that you finally put an end to the blocking policy in the field of data protection!
